```
Code for get_checkpoint:
```python
def get_checkpoint(prefix, epoch, ctx=mx.gpu(0), batch_size=32, image_size=299):
  height, width = image_size, image_size
  channels = 3
  sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint(prefix, epoch)
  mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=sym, context=ctx, label_names=None)
  mod.bind(for_training=False,
           data_shapes=[('data', (batch_size, channels, height, width))],
           label_shapes=mod._label_shapes)
  mod.set_params(arg_params, aux_params, allow_missing=True)
  return mod
```
Code for predict_on_rec_v1:
```python
def predict_on_rec_v1(model=None, path_imgrec=None, p_cutoff=0.9, ctx=mx.gpu(0), batch_size=32, data_shape=(3,299,299)):
  classIdx2label = {0:'cancer',1:'normal'}
  if path_imgrec is not None:
    data_iter = mx.io.ImageRecordIter(path_imgrec=path_imgrec, data_shape=data_shape, round_batch=False, \
                  batch_size=batch_size, mean_r=123.68, mean_g=116.779, mean_b=103.939, shuffle=False) # set round_batch=False
    p = model.predict(data_iter)
    p = p.asnumpy()
    k_cancer, k_normal = sum(p[:,0] > p_cutoff), sum(p[:,1] > p_cutoff)

    print("cancer vs. normal, {}, {} vs. {}.".format(os.path.basename(path_imgrec), \
          k_cancer, k_normal), file=sys.stderr)
    return p
  else:
    raise ValueError("path_imgrec is None.")
  return None
```
OS: ubuntu 16.04
mxnet package: mxnet_cu80-0.11.1b20170910-py2.py3-none-manylinux1_x86_64.whl

issue_title: Low accuracy of pvanet
issue_body:
I have changed a model which called pvanet from caffe [https://github.com/sanghoon/pva-faster-rcnn](https://github.com/sanghoon/pva-faster-rcnn), it is a implementation of the article by the author [https://arxiv.org/abs/1608.08021](https://arxiv.org/abs/1608.08021), my problem is why my accuracy is low than author's
What I have done:
1 Implement the classification net: [https://github.com/qingzhouzhen/incubator-mxnet/blob/pvanet-e2e/example/image-classification/symbols/pvanet.py](https://github.com/qingzhouzhen/incubator-mxnet/blob/pvanet-e2e/example/image-classification/symbols/pvanet.py)
2 feed the classification to Faster R-CNN module: [https://github.com/qingzhouzhen/incubator-mxnet/blob/pvanet-e2e/example/rcnn/rcnn/symbol/symbol_pvanet.py](https://github.com/qingzhouzhen/incubator-mxnet/blob/pvanet-e2e/example/rcnn/rcnn/symbol/symbol_pvanet.py)
Most job I have done is to atapt Faster-R-CNN,  replace the vgg pre-trained model with pvanet pre-trained model, for details see #7786 
Firstly the classification net pvanet's accuracy is 64.0%(70.6% as article)
Secondly detection net (pvanet+rpn+rcnn)'s mAP is 59.37%(82.5% as article)
I checked my net structure carefully to make it the same as author's implementation in caffe, is there anything I do wrong?

issue_title: [OP] LayerNorm in MXNet
issue_body:
## Description

[Layer Normalization](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1607.06450.pdf) is becoming more and more common in deep learning models, especially RNNs. We need to support the layer normalization layer. Although we can implement it by combining the existing symbols, e.g, [Implementation in Sockeye](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/master/sockeye/layers.py#L53-L112), the speed and memory cost would be large due to the various broadcasting operators that cannot be calculated inplace. Thus, we need to write our own C++ implementation of this operator.

Some reference implementations:
1. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/1959
2. https://github.com/MycChiu/fast-LayerNorm-TF

issue_title: The default weight initialization strategy makes the VGG network difficult to converge when utilizing examples under 'example/image-classification'
issue_body:
## Description
On trying to train CiFAR-10 dataset with VGG16, I noticed the initial learning rate has to be set a very small value to make the training converging. According the implementation of fit.py under example/image-classification/common, the Xavier with Gaussian random type is set as the default weight initializing strategy.

On a NVIDIA P40 GPU, with **CUDA-9.1** and **openblas**, I executed the following comparison tests:
Test 1: Initial LR = 0.01          Xavier with **_Gaussian_** random type
Test 2: Initial LR = 0.00025    Xavier with **_Gaussian_** random type
Test 3: Initial LR = 0.01          Xavier with **_uniform_** random type

The following two figures are corresponding to the comparison of the trends of training top-1 accuracy and cross-entropy loss. The curves in blue, green, purple are corresponding to test 1, 2, 3 respectively.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33112206/36579904-aae2350e-18a0-11e8-89aa-fc7e3876c0c3.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33112206/36579911-bdbcf1d2-18a0-11e8-8e02-d25a057bc56c.png)

It can be observed, with same initial LR (0.01), the training with Xavier-Gaussian will not converge.
Similar test results can also be observed when the dataset changed to CiFAR-100. (Retrieved from http://data.mxnet.io/data/cifar100.zip)  

## Environment info (Required)
HW: NVIDIA P40
SW:  MxNet master branch, with CUDA-v9.1, CUDNN v7.1 and openblas 2.1
 
What to do:
1. Download the diagnosis script from https://github.com/juliusshufan/mxnet 
2. Run the three python script correspond to test 1, 2, 3
(Note: The test script modified from the original train_cifar10.py coming with MXNET examples)

## Build info (Required if built from source)
Compiler: gcc 4.8.5
MXNet commit hash: cea8d2f4024a5e5d9d9edf43e42be130f10c7c27

Build config:
make -j($nproc) USE_OPENCV=1 USE_CUDA=1 USE_CUDNN=1 USE_BLAS=openblas USE_CUDA_PATH=/usr/local/cuda-9.1

## What have you tried to solve it?
Using the Xavier with uniform random, but not the Gaussian random.

issue_title: PyZMQ does not setup activation context for pyd extension on Windows
issue_body:
This is a bit of a long story, and it's not quite clear who's bug this is, but please bear with me while figuring this out.

On Windows, python and it's dependencies are generally linked against a specific named version of the MSCV C runtime (mscr90.dll). Making this work properly in general when parts of the application are using different C runtime versions requires a Side-by-Side manifest file. Libpython itself is built with such a manifest file and further, but extensions are generally not. Now, when libpython itself loads a `.pyd` extension, it extends its own activation context to the loaded extension [1], so everything is fine. 

Now, on Windows, pyzmq loads the `libzmq.pyd` python extension, but goes through ctypes [2] rather than the python extension mechanism, so the automatic extension of the activation context does not apply.

We encounter this in the python/julia bridge because julia is generally linked against a different C library.

Possible solutions:
1. Build libzmq.pyd with an appropriate manifest
2. Teach ctypes how to load libraries with the extended activation context.
3. Make ctypes handle the extended activation context by default for any shared library call
4. Don't use ctypes directly for loading libzmq.pyd (or at least load it via the standard extension mechanism first).
5. Work around this by setting the activation context manually in pyzmq
6. Work around this in the the embedded (in our case the python/julia bridge)

I will likely do approach 6 for the python/julia bridge to allow the basic use of `import zmq` to work, but I don't think it's the right long term solution (esp, because we can't really know when the right time to set the activation context is, so it will definitely be too broad). If approach 4 works, I think it would be a good fix to have in pyzmq (I wasn't able to figure out how to import a .pyd file given a file path, but would be happy to test if somebody can tell me). Approach 1 seems workable as well, but I don't know how that extension is built, so I'm not sure what it would take. Approaches 2/3 are more for the python/ctypes developers to think about, since I don't have any insight into what that would take.

Some references https://github.com/stevengj/PyCall.jl/issues/87 https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/17136 https://bugs.python.org/issue17213

[1] https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/c1ddef67a68a23e468984413b951097f8e2b63f3/Python/dynload_win.c#L216
[2]  https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/a960cc659c3e8deb83a9219059099de61d3e55f6/zmq/__init__.py#L37

issue_title: Inconsistent data types for send in the api
issue_body:
I am curious as to why the data types that the different zmq.send functions require are so different and I can not figure out the reason. As per the docs, zmq.send() requires data to be (object, str, Frame), then you have the function zmq.send_string() that requires the data to be a str (python3), but then zmq.send_multipart requires the data to be a list of (Frame, bytes, buffer-providers).

So send() accepts str but not bytes, then there is a method that accepts only str, and send_multipart() accepts bytes but not str. What is the reason for the api to be like this?

issue_title: Efficiently backfilling RedshiftQuery-based Tasks
issue_body:
We are currently exploring porting a fairly complex hourly Airflow DAG over to Luigi. The DAG consists of about 25 SubDAGs, which each contain 3 tasks that run SQL queries on Redshift. As part of moving, we want to backfill that task over the past 2 years. Doing this naively by having tasks depend on their previous hour obviously wouldn't work, as that would be ~1.3 MM tasks trying to be scheduled at once, so the plan is to do it in chunks, probably a week at a time. I've written the tasks so that their `DateHourParameter` has a `batch_method` of `list`, and they combine into a single query for each of the 3 subtasks. 

This all works fine, if I stub out the completeness checks. The issue lies in that using Redshift as the backing store for the marker_table is prohibitively slow. `luigi-deps-tree` using RedshiftQuery takes 20 seconds for 1 hour, where with PostgresQuery it takes 300ms.

One solution is to use `luigi.tools.RangeHourly`, but the issue is this only works with the first task, not for all of it's dependencies. Is the proper direction here to extend `RangeHourlyBase` in all of the tasks and pass along the `RangeHourlyBase` parameters using `of_params`?

Another solution would be to use something else as the backing store for `RedshiftQuery` completeness checks, but it doesn't feel right to have `RedshiftTarget.exists()` check something besides Redshift, and we'd lose the benefit of updates to the table running in the same transaction as the query itself. All those aside, this seems like one of the easier solutions.

The only other solution I can think of, is to move `Task.bulk_complete` into luigi core, with a default impl that naively checks each `Task(...).complete()` individually, and use that everywhere possible in the worker. This is definitely the most involved solution, but I imagine that there are a lot of situations where this would lead to faster scheduling, and it would probably be the most beneficial to the ecosystem as a whole. However, as we aren't totally invested in Luigi yet, it's also the hardest sell.

Before I started down any of those three roads I was hoping I could get some feedback on the proposed solutions.

issue_title: Assistant dynamic resources
issue_body:
**Use case**: Spawn new assistant instances on-the-fly (scale up/down) and connect them to the central planner
**Issue**: Resource pool is allocated when central planner is starting via configuration file. Dynamic resource pool modification is currently not possible from the Luigi client
**Solution**: It would be nice to be able to push new host-based resources pool dynamically when a new assistant is launched. For example,

Assistant A has 8 resources available
Assistant B has 6 resources available
Assistant C has 2 resources available

Task X requires 4 resources
Task Y requires 1 resources

Scheduler should be smart enough to schedule X on A or B (but not C) and Y on A, B or C. Lets spawn a new assistant

Assistant D has 12 resources available

The Luigi client should be able to notify the central planner that new resources are available and start scheduling jobs on it. The same mechanic applies when an assistant goes offline.

This is mostly food for tough and I would like the community's opinion on such a feature.

issue_title: MissingParameterException when generating execution summary
issue_body:
Generating the execution summary may generate a MissingParameterException if a `requires` attempts to access a Config parameter which was specified only on the command line.

Example:

```python
# trouble.py
import luigi

class conf(luigi.Config):
    opt = luigi.Parameter()

class Root(luigi.WrapperTask):
    def requires(self):
        if conf().opt == 'A':
            return A()

class A(luigi.Task):
    def complete(self):
        return False

    def run(self):
        raise Exception()
```

```
$ luigi --module trouble Root --conf-opt=A
===== Luigi Execution Summary =====

Scheduled 2 tasks of which:
* 1 failed:
    - 1 A()
* 1 were left pending, among these:
    * 1 had failed dependencies:

issue_title: Improve boto3 client publish documentation
issue_body:
I spent a lot of time due to misinformation on the wiki page dealing with the sns clients publish API. 
[the boto3 SNS documentation of the publish() function][1] is pretty confusing:

> If you want to send the same message to **all transport protocols**, include the text of the message as a String value.

Wrong, GCM, a transport protocol that nearly all android devices use, does not work this way! Maybe this should be mentioned.

> If you want to send different messages for each transport protocol, set the value of the MessageStructure parameter to json **and use a JSON object for the Message parameter**.

No, you can't use a JSON object for the Message parameter. The parameter still needs to be a string object. But it needs to contain parsable JSON. This should be reworded.

> the value of the Message parameter must:

> * be a syntactically valid JSON object; and

> * contain at least a top-level JSON key of "default" with a value that is a string.

Neither point is true.

Also, the fact that you need another `json.dumps()` within the JSON object is mentioned nowhere. A simple example like the one above would have helped wonders in that documentation.


Could this be improved?


  [1]: http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/sns.html#SNS.Client.publish

issue_title: Clients are never GC'd
issue_body:
```python
def __del__(obj):
    print "deleting", obj

class foo(object):
    pass

def main():
    import botocore.session
    session = botocore.session.get_session()
    session.__class__.__del__ = __del__
    client = session.create_client('s3', region_name='us-west-2')
    client.__class__.__del__ = __del__

    thing = foo()
    thing.__class__.__del__ = __del__

print "before"
main()
print "after"
```

Prints:

```
before
deleting <botocore.session.Session object at 0x7fd23c7ec550>
deleting <__main__.foo object at 0x7fd23c7a4110>
after

This is causing major memory leaks in our applications that create lots of clients.

issue_title: Creating Resource Pool Results in 0 Resource Capacity on New Pool
issue_body:
Running vim.Folder().CreateResourcePool() on an existing vim.Folder object works successfully, however I stumbled upon not being able to start any virtual machines in resource pools I create with this method. Manually creating the pools with the same settings in vsphere web client results in the desired behavior. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7663028/31414315-946b9666-adda-11e7-8d55-7773299336b0.png)
As you can see, this resource pool has 0 available resources in either capacity or free space. I have to manually re-create this respool to fix the issue. 
This is the function I wrote to create a resource pool:
```python
def create_student_respool(username):
    content = si.RetrieveContent()
    computeConsumer = get_role(content, "Compute Consumer")
    users = get_obj(content, [vim.ResourcePool], "users")
    configSpec = vim.ResourceConfigSpec()
    sharesInfo = vim.SharesInfo(level='normal')
    cpuAllocationInfo = vim.ResourceAllocationInfo()
    memAllocationInfo = vim.ResourceAllocationInfo()
    
    cpuAllocationInfo.reservation = 0
    cpuAllocationInfo.expandableReservation = False
    cpuAllocationInfo.limit = 20000  # 20GHz
    cpuAllocationInfo.shares = sharesInfo
    
    
    memAllocationInfo.reservation = 0
    memAllocationInfo.expandableReservation = False
    memAllocationInfo.limit = 8192  # 8GB
    memAllocationInfo.shares = sharesInfo
    
    configSpec.cpuAllocation = cpuAllocationInfo
    configSpec.memoryAllocation = memAllocationInfo
    new_pool = users.CreateResourcePool(username, configSpec)
    new_perms = [
        vim.AuthorizationManager.Permission(
            entity=new_pool,
            principal="CRL\\{}".format(username),
            group = False, 
            roleId = computeConsumer.roleId, 
            propagate=True
        )
    ]
    content.authorizationManager.SetEntityPermissions(new_pool, new_perms)
    return new_pool
```
I'm also setting permissions on the resource pool using my own predefined role and a user principal name that corresponds to an Active Directory user that I specify.

Am I missing a field in the configSpec? or does this look like a bug?

issue_title: Invalid type comment: (method-wrapper)
issue_body:
**python version:** Python 3.6.3 (CPython)
**pyannonate version:** 1.0.0

I get the following trace when I try to run the `pyannonate` CLI on a generated JSON file.

```python
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/bin/pyannotate", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyannotate_tools/annotations/__main__.py", line 45, in main
    generate_annotations_json(infile, tf.name)
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyannotate_tools/annotations/main.py", line 37, in generate_annotations_json
    arg_types, return_type = infer_annotation(item.type_comments)
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyannotate_tools/annotations/infer.py", line 38, in infer_annotation
    arg_types, return_type = parse_type_comment(comment)
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyannotate_tools/annotations/parse.py", line 196, in parse_type_comment
    return Parser(comment).parse()
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyannotate_tools/annotations/parse.py", line 205, in __init__
    self.tokens = tokenize(comment)
  File "/home/kura/.virtualenvs/blackhole/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyannotate_tools/annotations/parse.py", line 188, in tokenize
    raise ParseError(original)
pyannotate_tools.annotations.parse.ParseError: Invalid type comment: (method-wrapper) -> bool
```

@gvanrossum commented that this is an issue with the naming of an internal type -- https://github.com/dropbox/pyannotate/issues/4#issuecomment-345344348

The offending piece of my code that generates this internal type name is below.

```python
def validate_option(self, key):
    """
    Validate config option is actually... valid...

    https://kura.github.io/blackhole/configuration.html#configuration-options

    :param str key: Configuration option.
    :raises ConfigException: When an invalid option is configured.
    """
    if key == '':
        return
    attributes = inspect.getmembers(self,
                                    lambda a: not(inspect.isroutine(a)))
    attrs = [a[0][1:] for a in attributes if not(a[0].startswith('__') and
             a[0].endswith('__')) and a[0].startswith('_')]
    if key not in attrs:
        valid_attrs = ('\'{0}\' and '
                       '\'{1}\'').format('\', \''.join(attrs[:-1]),
                                         attrs[-1])
        msg = ('Invalid configuration option \'{0}\'.\n\nValid options '
               'are: {1}'.format(key, valid_attrs))
        raise ConfigException(msg)
```

Specifically, the line that generates the `method-wrapper` type contains the `inspect.getmembers` call with a `lambda` as an argument.

```python
attributes = inspect.getmembers(self, lambda a: not(inspect.isroutine(a)))
```

Which generates the following piece of JSON.

```json
{
    "path": "blackhole/config.py",
    "line": 247,
    "func_name": "<lambda>",
    "type_comments": [
        "(str) -> bool",
        "(method-wrapper) -> bool",
        "(builtin_function_or_method) -> bool",
        "(blackhole.utils.Singleton) -> bool",
        "(bool) -> bool",
        "(method) -> bool",
        "(None) -> bool",
        "(Dict[str, Union[pathlib.PurePosixPath, str]]) -> bool"
    ],
    "samples": 954
},
```

Hope that helps.

issue_title: Allow selection of points in Map
issue_body:
Rendering points as output of calculations and other processing is a good first step.

A user should be able to select points, and groups of points, for further analysis. This is similar to the `Keep Only` or `Exclude` functions that are used in Tableau's mapping functions. 

Kept (selected data) or Excluded (All - Selected) should then be exported back to a DF for additional analysis.

issue_title: Streamline authentication
issue_body:
It would be helpful to make authenticating to restricted TDS instances (like from ESGF) simpler. This could be used to try to script up access to CMIP5 data:

>I was able to generate a certificate using the following syntax in terminal;

```sh
myproxyclient logon -b true -T true -s pcmdi.llnl.gov -l jsimkins2 -o ~/creds.pem
```

with the python package myproxyclient and other directions found [here.](https://dev.knmi.nl/projects/impactportal/wiki/Commandline_access_to_secured_ESGF_NetCDF_files)

>Although I was able to generate the certificate, the ESGF server didn't accept it. I tried the methods
>in the link above but was unsuccessful. After working with the scientists from ESGF, it seems as if
>the Certificate Authentication authority wouldn't accept the certificates generated by any user and
>this problem exists on their end to my knowledge. This was about a month ago so perhaps things
>have changed now that I brought this to their attention. It would be fantastic if Siphon could be used
>to access guarded datasets like CMIP5. I hope this gives you an idea of how far I got with the issue
>and hopefully Siphon can carry the torch from here.

>My intention with getting this to work is to create a download function that can extract CMIP5
>outputs for a single grid point using OPeNDAP/THREDDS. Hopefully there is a way around the
>token based authentication.

issue_title: Delete Pipfile.lock as late as possible
issue_body:
When updating Pipfile.lock, the old version is deleted before anything happens.  If the update process fails (maybe because one Ctrl+C's it because of a mistake, or it's [taking too long][3], or [it crashes][4], etc.), then one is left with no lockfile.

Could the behaviour be changed so that the [lockfile deletion][1] is performed **immediately** before the [serialisation of the new content][2]?  (FWIW, I'd be happy to attempt a PR for this.)

I guess two rationales for the current behaviour:

1. Your `Pipfile.lock` should be in Git.  Yes, but it still sucks if you're doing a sequence of new package installs/etc. and haven't committed your work.
2. The update of the `Pipfile.lock` should be consistent with the current state of the virtualenv.  I'd buy that, but the current behaviour (no lockfile) doesn't strike me as better :wink:.

[1]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/core.py#L1026
[2]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/core.py#L1173
[3]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1785
[4]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1837

------------

##### Steps to replicate

1. Start with an existing Pipenv environment (i.e. you have a working `Pipfile`/`Pipfile.lock`).
2. Run `pipenv install <any_dep_that_takes_a_long_time>`.
3. Hit Ctrl+C before it's finished.

issue_title: package install ends with temp files not deleted
issue_body:
When `pipenv install` finishes installing packages there are leftovers in `/tmp/pipenv-xxxxx-requirement.txt` (one per package installed).

##### Describe your environment

1. `Linux 4.13.0-36-generic #40-Ubuntu SMP x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux`
2. Python version: tested with 2.7.14 and 3.6.4
3. Pipenv version: 10.1.2

##### Expected result

no tmp files left

##### Actual result

many tmp files in /tmp 

##### Steps to replicate

just install any package

The issue is caused IMO by running pipenv concurrently, the tmp file cannot be immediately deleted [at this point](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/5ba8c9cf2484d257c21ded9eeca7f981bc911d0a/pipenv/core.py#L1465). 

I think we can add a small cleanup task similar to `pipenv.utils.rmtree()` at `pipenv/core.py +890` when we're sure that the job is finished.
A couple of options for this:

1) `pipenv.core.pip_install()` returns also a list of files that has used but not deleted. Our cleanup task takes it as input and does the cleanup at the very end of `pipenv.core.do_install_dependencies()` before returning.

2) Or  one can amend the `project.Project` adding the temp dir where the requirement files are created so the cleanup task can be executed with something like `rmtree(project.tmp_requirements_location)`.

Any thoughts on this issue?

If you agree with the proposed solution, I can submit a patch for evaluation. 

Thanks!

issue_title: Document Markers ("---" / "...") handling.
issue_body:
Hello.

I think i've found a small deviation from the YAML specs.
in  [9.1.2. Document Markers](http://yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2800401)

The spec says: ```Obviously, the actual content lines are therefore forbidden to begin with either of these markers.```

Yet On the JS-YAML online demo the following sample is **valid**:
```yaml
---
foo : 1
bar : 2
...foo : 3
---bar : 4
...
```

Additionally there seems to be limitations on the use of indentation with Document Markers.

A '---' marker cannot be preceded by indentation.
```yaml
      --- #INVALID
foo : 1
bar : 2
...
```

And if a "..." marker is preceded by indentation it becomes parts of a multi line scalar.
```yaml
---
foo : 1
bar : 2
  ... 
# will output { foo: 1, bar: '2 ...' }
```

Is my understanding of the spec correct?

issue_title: Rdkafka cannot accept message sizes larger than default
issue_body:
Hi,

There's a bug in the Rdkafka producer code.

First, I configure my broker to accept large messages (with all the proper configs, including message.max.bytes, fetch.message.max.bytes, replica.fetch.max.bytes, socket.send.buffer.bytes, and socket.receive.buffer.bytes). Then I setup an Rdkafka producer and try to send large messages but this fails because the option `max_request_size` does not exist for the Rdkafka producer, and this option is used to set the message.max.bytes config for Rdkafka itself.

Looking at the code, the fix is extremely simple: just add max_request_size to the arguments of the Rdkafka producer constructor and It Just Works as long as I set it to be larger than my max message sizes.

Will try to figure out the contribution process here soon and contribute the pull request, but just wanted to make sure this didn't slip through the cracks.

issue_title: Please do not use Broker-side exceptions for Client-side errors
issue_body:
When debugging https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/696 where the broker successfully returns a message to the consumer and then the consumer blows up, I was confused because the error that was raised was `MessageSizeTooLarge` which is a broker error code number 10: https://kafka.apache.org/protocol.html#protocol_error_codes

This was extremely confusing to me because initial spelunking in the pykafka source showed that this exception subclassed `ProtocolClientError`, indicating that the broker was returning the error code:
https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/c73f060abbf46f81ccc42798e5dc7e55bb3c65a2/pykafka/exceptions.py#L138

Furthermore, it made no sense contextually because that error code is only emitted by the broker to a producer trying to produce too large of a message, not to a consumer.

It had me scratching my head until I realized that `pykafka` was re-using what is semantically a broker-side exception for a client-side error:  https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blame/c80f66c0d0b11d830aa333ed486967b5f242bc2f/pykafka/protocol.py#L383

Digging a little deeper, I see that originally this exception wasn't the same as a broker-side error, but in #500 @emmett9001 changed it to re-use that exception class based on discussion in #497.

Can I request that you limit the use of broker-side exceptions to only situations where the broker is actually returning an error code?

In this particular case, the proper fix is probably removing this particular usage of the exception altogether as suggested in #696. If you choose to keep it a hard-limit and throw an exception, can you use an exception that more clearly indicates a client-side error? Probably with a suggestion to the user to increase their `fetch_message_max_bytes` setting.

issue_title: error reporting from reset_offsets
issue_body: [Here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/pull/194#discussion_r33518462), we don't raise an exception if all attempts at `reset_offsets` fail. Thus, the only way for the client to know that it failed is to check the offsets stored in the consumer. We can't simply raise an exception, since in cases where `reset_offsets` is called in response to another error, we'd like it to silently fail.

issue_title: skimage.color.rgb2ycbcr results in wrong value (modified)
issue_body:
I have got the solution by myself, please see the second comment, thanks a lot.

original problem:
==========================================
Hi guys, I found an wrong result in the function rgb2ycbcr (in version maybe pre-0.13?).
as the code in line 1687:
```
    arr = _convert(ycbcr_from_rgb, rgb)
    arr[..., 0] += 16
    arr[..., 1] += 128
    arr[..., 2] += 128
    return arr
```
however,  it will call the function `_prepare_colorarray()` in `_convert()`, as line in 157:
```
return dtype.img_as_float(arr)
```
causing the value scaling in [0,1] before adding the bias.

I suggest that you should do scaling them back to [0,255], just simple way as below or others:
```
    arr = _convert(ycbcr_from_rgb, rgb)
+   arr = arr * 255.0
    arr[..., 0] += 16
    arr[..., 1] += 128
    arr[..., 2] += 128
    return arr
```

I try this code with JPEG conversion, which Y is in [0,255], and get a correct answer same with using Pillow.
```
    arr = _convert(ycbcr_from_rgb, rgb)
+   arr = arr * 255.0
    arr[..., 0] += 0
    arr[..., 1] += 128
    arr[..., 2] += 128
    return arr
```
I'm not sure whether it is correct in your ITU-R BT.601 conversion, maybe you should recheck it again.

issue_title: Move registerBuildInRegion call in nupic.engine.__init__ to global scope
issue_body: `registerBuildInRegion` needs to be run before any of the methods in `nupic.engine.__init__` are run. Otherwise, if `registerPyRegion` or `unregisterPyRegion` is run before a network is initialized (as would happen if loading a serialized network with regions that conflict with the built ins), `registerBuildInRegion` would either break or not have the proper region in it's list of regions.

issue_title: WebMessage: find recipients on more criteria than just nickname
issue_body:
Originally on 2013-06-20

When preparing a message, WebMessage allows the sender to search for users registered in the system using their nickname (https://invenio-demo.cern.ch/yourmessages/write). It might be however that a nickname does not exist for some users, for eg. when using external authentication systems (for eg. with `external_authentication_sso` when initialized with `enforce_external_nicknames=False`). It becomes then impossible to select these users as recipients, unless they specifically set a nickname in their settings. The same issue extends to "WebGroup", which uses WebMessage as a way to send invitation to join a group.

One possible solution would be to also search in the list of registered emails (and use these emails instead of missing nicknames everywhere needed). One drawback is that it gives access to the list of emails of registered users, which one might not want to expose (For eg. spamming. For eg. tendering and bidding processes at CERN might requires that competitors are not known, etc.). It would not be possible to strip out the domain part of the email, as the prefix would not always be unique.

Another possibility would be to force the construction of unique nicknames, based on the users email prefix (and resolve conflicts by adding an integer) when the external authentication system is not providing one. That might have however deeper consequences in the system.

An alternative would be to simply (optionally) ask the user to fill in their nickname the first time they log in through an external authentication system that does not provide a value for the nickname. It could simply direct to the his/her account page ([YourAccount](https://invenio-demo.cern.ch/youraccount/display)) with a message suggesting to set a nickname (with an explanation about the reason for such a nickname), in the same way as message are displayed there to the admin when an Invenio upgrade is available.

Please propose alternatives...

One could also consider this behavior to be a feature, which allows users to choose to remain unreachable/unknown to other users, unless they opt-in (by setting a nickname).

issue_title: RFC removal of robotlogin
issue_body:
Robot login is a proprietary authentication protocol (similar to OAUTH), initially developed for the *OpenAIRE Orphan Repository* to exploit the *OpenAIRE Portal* for authentication, and later adopted by the *INSPIRE* service to exploit *arXiv.org* for authentication.

Today the OpenAIRE Orphan Repository has been replaced by Zenodo, and INSPIRE is little by little upgraded to INSPIRE Labs which sports native authentication (OAUTH-based) from ORCID.

Therefore I'd propose to deprecate it.

INSPIRE will be able to still import data from arXiv using a dedicated module.

issue_title: docker: common/dev/debug/prod configurations
issue_body:
Currently, we advertise how to develop Invenio 2.x via Docker in our documentation, using DEV configuration. However, when somebody goes that way, he/she would find that running test suite reports many failures due to HTTP redirection associated with debugging mode.  This may be a rather surprising default...

I'd rather propose to strive at the defaults whether the following would hold true:

```
$ git checkout maint-2.0
$ docker-compose build
$ docker-compose up
$ docker exec -i -t invenio_web_1 python setup.py test

[...]

Ran 977 tests in 701.359s

OK (skipped=6)
```

(This is the case for Invenio 1.x Docker configurations, for our Flask-* and other projects...)

We could achieve this by reconsidering DEV -> DEBUG -> PROD modes, choosing a conservative default, and [extending our configurations progressively](https://blog.docker.com/2015/04/easily-configure-apps-for-multiple-environments-with-compose-1-2-and-much-more/) for various use case scenarios. For example:

- COMMON profile: create common Docker Compose configuration as `docker-compose-common.yml` that will be used as a base for everything;
- DEV profile [*default*]: create `docker-compose.yml` that will extend COMMON to make Invenio runnable in a DEV friendly mode, without extra debugging and stuff, but mirroring what Travis/Jenkins do; useful for regular CI/kwalitee purposes;
- DEBUG profile: if wanted, create another `docker-compose-debug.yml` that would extend COMMON/DEV with human-related DEBUG capabilities, with Flask-DebugToolbar/Werkzeug and friends on; either create new profile if needed or just pass appropriate environment variables to the DEV profile to achieve the same;
- PROD profile: create yet another `docker-compose-production.yml` that will extend COMMON in a way that will be useful for composing production Invenio environments with overlays. (TBD)

WDYT? CC @crepererum @jirikuncar

issue_title: CentOS 6 and Docker
issue_body:
Hi.
I tried to install invenio 1.2.2 on CentOS 6 using Docker (the dockerfile says "
_Using CentOS-6 with Python-2.6 and MySQL-5.1, fitting our minimal
requirements for Invenio v1.2:
FROM centos:6."_

My first problem was the installation of Docker on CentOS 6 (on the docker official webpage we can read that "Docker runs on CentOS 7.X").
CentOS 6 runs on kernel 2.6, which is at least 13 years old now. That kernel misses various things needed to run Docker. Some users have had problems updating the kernel because "Docker requires a 64-bit OS and version 3.10 or higher of the Linux kernel". 

So, if possible, I have two requests:
a) Update the dockerfile configuration according to CentOS 7 or
b) Configure the dockerfile for debian/ubuntu (as we can do with Invenio 3.0)

Thanks for your help.

J. Martin

issue_title: tests: simplify doctest execution
issue_labels: [{'id': 414890435, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-mail/labels/Size:%20easy', 'name': 'Size: easy', 'color': 'bc3cbc', 'default': False}, {'id': 414890375, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-mail/labels/Status:%20ready%20for%20work', 'name': 'Status: ready for work', 'color': '149414', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: Reach 100% coverage
Docs to RTD
Reach 100% coverage
Support Mandrill's Authentication
issue_body:
Mandrill supplies a header `X-Mandrill-Signature` to [authenticate their requests](https://mandrill.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/205583257-How-to-Authenticate-Webhook-Requests). Inbound Email should be able to support it by adding one setting to determine the webhook url.

Mandrill's signature calculation is as follows:

1. Create a string with the webhook's URL, exactly as you entered it in Mandrill (including any query strings, if applicable). Mandrill always signs webhook requests with the exact URL you provided when you configured the webhook. A difference as small as including or removing a trailing slash will prevent the signature from validating.
2. Sort the request's POST variables alphabetically by key.
3. Append each POST variable's key and value to the URL string, with no delimiter.
4. Hash the resulting string with HMAC-SHA1, using your webhook's authentication key to generate a binary signature.
5. Base64 encode the binary signature
6. Compare the binary signature that you generated to the signature provided in the X-Mandrill-Signature HTTP header.

PR to follow.

issue_title: Messages with only HTML (and no plain text) content from Sendgrid are rejected
issue_body:
Hi there,

It's took me a bit of time to track down this problem with the package. In the `sendgrid.py` backend, text content is required, while HTML is optional:
`            text = _decode_POST_value(request, 'text')`
`            html = _decode_POST_value(request, 'html', default='')`

This means that any message with only HTML encoded text is rejected by the server. If `text` is changed to include a default, like this: 
`            text = _decode_POST_value(request, 'text', default='')`

Then any type of message can be received.

Not sure if it's easier for me to do a PR for this, or just make the quick change when you're doing other tests, but I'd be happy to. Not sure if this is an issue the other backends.

Cheers

issue_title: Security vulnerability that allows hijacking another user's account
issue_body:
Django REST Framework JWT are vulnerable to a username hijacking attack, in which one user can gain access to another user's account. This occurs because the JWT token identifies the user's account using it's username (which could change) rather than User.pk (the user record's primary key, which is reliable and always points to the same user account. Here's one example (out of many) on how this exploit could be performed:

Step 1: I create account with username "X", create a JWT token, then rename my account to "Y".
Step 2: Someone else creates account with username "X".
Step 3: I access the other person's account using my JWT token.

Setting a short expiration window into the JWT token doesn't eliminate this threat vector, because a malicious user could design an attack that performs step 1 shortly before step 2 occurs.

The fix is to have the JWT token identify the user account being authenticated using a value that is immutable, such as the pk of the User record (which is id by default, but could be implemented as a uuid). Usernames are a bad choice for long-term identification of users because it's common for websites to allow users to change their usernames.

issue_title: CircleCI + KeenClient-Python
issue_body:
When installing a recent keen python client on CircleCI under python 2.7.9, I ran into an error.

 Circle's 2.7.9 virtualenv is using pip 1.4.1, which is truly ancient, but CircleCI is a pretty popular build environment.  I think an appropriate fix would either be to try/except in the setup.py around parse_requirements, or to document this specific error with the workaround suggestion of upgrading CircleCI's install pip.

I'll comment below with the original build error as well as the workaround that worked for me.

Alternatively, I think this would work around the problem in setup.py:

```
try: 
    install_reqs = parse_requirements('requirements.txt', session=pip.download.PipSession())
except AttributeError:
    # compatibility for pip < 1.5.6 (which CircleCI uses as of 20150401)
    install_reqs = parse_requirements('requirements.txt')
```

issue_title: `IndexError: pop from empty list` on request finished signal handler
issue_body:
Raven-Python 6.0.0 on Django 1.9.13

All affected requests are `HEAD`. But not all `HEAD` requests are affected. Neither all of the exact same requests(Uptime tracking system) are affected.

```
IndexError: pop from empty list
  File "raven/middleware.py", line 20, in common_exception_handling
    yield
  File "raven/middleware.py", line 71, in close
    self._close()
  File "django/http/response.py", line 252, in close
    signals.request_finished.send(sender=self._handler_class)
  File "raven/contrib/django/middleware/__init__.py", line 111, in request_finished
    client.transaction.pop(self._txid)
  File "raven/utils/transaction.py", line 47, in pop
    return self.stack.pop()
```

Could this be caused by a `request_finished` sent without having the prerequisite `request_started` ? I suspect a premature close of the TCP/HTTP connection causing the request cycle behave in a weird manner.

On a 7days range, this error happens on few candidates (<10) over a very large sample (100,000+) of **identical** requests spread across tens of dockers hosts running the same codebase.

![2017-05-05 at 7 29 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/474697/25744025/9e3f07c8-3165-11e7-94df-cb726fbda4c7.png)
![2017-05-05 at 7 44 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/474697/25744247/bfd5a51c-3166-11e7-987b-f1c0e76e359f.png)

issue_title: Give photons plausible incidence angles
issue_body:
To properly rain electrons through a detector, they should really have the right distribution of incidence angles, so the different wavelengths that convert to electrons at different depth have the right spatial positions.

This issue is to add a function to give a PhotonArray with a bunch of photons values for the incidence angles given some f/ratio and obscuration fraction.

The assumption is that photons are uniformly distributed within the non-obscured solid angle.

I propose that our "angles" really be dx/dz and dy/dz values giving the slope in x or y relative to the motion in z.  This will likely save us from having trig calls when we actually want to use the angles to compute something.

issue_title: Icon Request: icon-name
Resources with a "?" in the URL are not cached by some proxy caching servers
issue_body:
Hi,

About your _font-awesome.min.css_ file:
`font-face{font-family:'FontAwesome';
src:url('../fonts/fontawesome-webfont.eot?v=4.7.0');
src:url('../fonts/fontawesome-webfont.eot?#iefix&v=4.7.0') format('embedded-opentype'),
url('../fonts/fontawesome-webfont.woff2?v=4.7.0') format('woff2'),
url('../fonts/fontawesome-webfont.woff?v=4.7.0')
`

Speedtest website Pingdom.com explain that the lines with ?v=4.7.0 should be removed if possible. Since these links may not be cached by some proxies servers:

> Resources with a "?" in the URL are not cached by some proxy caching servers. Remove the query string and encode the parameters into the URL for the following resources:
> 
>     https://maxcdn.bootstrapcdn.com/font-awesome/4.7.0/fonts/fontawesome-webfont.woff2?v=4.7.0
> 

Do you agree with this remark? If so, you may want to fix this.

Kind regards,
Melroy

issue_title: Remove sdict library
issue_body:
The requirement `dict.sorted` doesn't have a vendored rpm package associated with it, and it is the only one of our libraries like this. It seems that it's not required for any obvious reason, and we have an ordered dump of YAML in the works right now, which might serve our purposes whenever we need to preserve order.

This issue is to remove the package and work around any problems that come up doing so.

issue_title: File upload with multipart/form-data not handled properly
issue_body:
### Description
I noticed weird behavior when uploading a file through multipart/form-data field: if by mistake client uploads URL instead of file bytes, server crashes, thus returning 500 error. Since it was a malformed request, it should return 400.


### Expected behaviour
Return 400 bad request error.


### Actual behaviour
Returns 500 error.


### Steps to reproduce

#### yaml config

```
/endpoint/:
    post:
      operationId: app.execute
      parameters:

        - in: formData
          name: image
          description: image to be processed
          type: file
          required: false
```

#### request

`curl -X POST   http://127.0.0.1:8000/endpoint/  -F image=https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7d/Lenna_%28test_image%29.png/220px-Lenna_%28test_image%29.png`

#### response

500

#### server stack trace

```
KeyError: 'file'
	at make_type (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/parameter.py:56)
	at get_val_from_param (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/parameter.py:67)
	at wrapper (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/parameter.py:181)
	at wrapper (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py:44)
	at wrapper (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/validation.py:300)
	at wrapper (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/uri_parsing.py:117)
	at wrapper (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py:73)
	at dispatch_request (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py:1799)
	at full_dispatch_request (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py:1813)
	at reraise (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/_compat.py:35)
	at handle_user_exception (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py:1718)
	at wrapped_function (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask_cors/extension.py:161)
	at full_dispatch_request (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py:1815)
	at wsgi_app (/app/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py:2292)
```



### Additional info:

It is likely caused by uploaded files being passed in form data, instead of requests.files: https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/parameter.py#L168-L190.

issue_title: Make it easier to use OpenFisca in an hostile environnement
issue_body:
By hostile we mean:

- Windows (possibly old versions)
- Limited access to the internet 
- No administrator privilege on the computer

OpenFisca is a software targeted at administrations. We cannot expect our customers to have a proper development environment. 

Efforts have already been made in the past to address this issue:
  - Publish of Windows-compatible wheels on pypi
  - Offer a notebook server _(discontinued, as it costed too much time to maintain it)_
  - Explore the [binder](http://mybinder.org/) solution  (@edarin)

However, the training at the French Senate showed that we still had progress to do.

issue_title: Imports fail for non-org users
issue_body:
If you try to import a dataset with a non-organization user, it imports the dataset correctly, but then it raises an error:

```python
from carto.auth import APIKeyAuthClient
from carto.datasets import DatasetManager
auth_client = APIKeyAuthClient(api_key="APIKEY", base_url="https://NONORGUSER.carto.com/")
dataset_manager = DatasetManager(auth_client)
dataset = dataset_manager.create(DATAURL)
```

This is the error (I've omitted non-relevant information).

```
.../carto-python/carto/resources.py:90: FutureWarning: This is part of a non-public CARTO API and may change in the future. Take this into account if you are using this in a production environment
  warnings.warn('This is part of a non-public CARTO API and may change in the future. Take this into account if you are using this in a production environment', FutureWarning)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File ".../carto-python/carto/datasets.py", line 213, in create
    table = TableManager(self.client).get(import_job.table_id)
...
  File ".../carto-python/carto/users.py", line 64, in __init__
    organization-enabled APIKeyAuthClient"))
carto.exceptions.CartoException: User management requires an                                    organization-enabled APIKeyAuthClient
```
The problem seems to be that, after the import, the table information is requested (with `Table` class) which contains a `permission` (`PermissionField`class) which in turn has an owner ( `User`class), But the User class only support organization users.

I've managed to avoid the problem with this patch:

```diff
diff --git a/carto/users.py b/carto/users.py
index f23f12a..b1e89e3 100644
--- a/carto/users.py
+++ b/carto/users.py
@@ -26,6 +26,7 @@ from .resources import Manager, WarnResource

 API_VERSION = "v1"
 API_ENDPOINT = "api/{api_version}/organization/{organization}/users/"
+API_NONORG_ENDPOINT = "api/{api_version}/users/"


 class User(WarnResource):
@@ -60,15 +61,16 @@ class User(WarnResource):
         :param auth_client: Auth client
         """
         if auth_client.organization is None:
-            raise CartoException(_("User management requires an \
-                                   organization-enabled APIKeyAuthClient"))
+            self._api_endpoint = API_NONORG_ENDPOINT
+        else:
+            self._api_endpoint = API_ENDPOINT

         super(User, self).__init__(auth_client)

     def get_collection_endpoint(self):
         """
         """
-        return API_ENDPOINT.format(api_version=API_VERSION,
+        return self._api_endpoint.format(api_version=API_VERSION,
                                    organization=self.client.organization)

     def get_resource_endpoint(self):
@@ -100,7 +102,7 @@ class UserManager(Manager):
     def get_collection_endpoint(self):
         """
         """
-        return API_ENDPOINT.format(api_version=API_VERSION,
+        return self._api_endpoint.format(api_version=API_VERSION,
                                    organization=self.client.organization)

     def get_resource_endpoint(self, resource_id):
```

issue_title: User name on `import_and_merge` should be optional
issue_body: as with the rest of carto credential details, the user name should be taken as an option using an environment variable as a default like `CARTO_USER`

issue_title: Resource.__getattr__() may fall-through without returning a value
issue_body:
`Resource`'s `__getattr__` method currently allows a fall-through and implicit `None` return.

e.g. `issue.any_propery_that_does_not_exist` evaluates to `None`.

This is not helpful. An `AttributeError` should be raised when the attribute is not found.

issue_title: Why is the requests requirement so high?
issue_body:
Requests is currently set at 2.10. Is there any reason for this? 

As far as I can tell--both from the code and from some testing I've done--it could be much lower. A lot of people are still using lower versions of requests in their apps.

Is there any particular reason why it's so high?

issue_title: set_credentials and friends should add connect arg
issue_body:
Currently when you use set_credentials and specify to authenticate, it runs _auth_and_connect without the ability to specify to not connect.  If you use set_credentials when attempting to connect to a non-Rackspace OpenStack environment, it immediately tries to run the connect_to_\* helpers, which is likely not what you want.

We should add a connect argument to these functions, to prevent connecting to the Rackspace services.

Another option is to rethink how the set_credentials, auth and connect stuff works, to make it a little more OpenStack friendly, while still giving developers easy access to use the existing functions/methods.

typing==3.6.2
unicorn==1.0.1
urllib3==1.22
wcwidth==0.1.7
z3-solver==4.5.1.0.post2

### Summary of the problem

While testing on a simple target program it takes about 1m35s to reach the `main` function. Most of this time seems to be spent loading libc and I get similar behavior regardless of whether I use the API programatically or via the `manticore` tool. 

The main issue with this is that it makes iterative development of a script based on manticore quite painful as every run has an overhead of at least 1m30s, regardless of how complex the target is. 

### Step to reproduce the behavior

Timing script: 
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

import sys
import time

from manticore import Manticore

# The address of the main() function
MAIN_ADDR = 0x4005b6
start_time = time.time()
m = Manticore(sys.argv[1])

@m.hook(MAIN_ADDR)
def log_msg(state):
    print "Reached main after %f seconds" % (time.time() - start_time)
    m.terminate()

m.run(procs=1)
```

Test program:
```
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
    if (argc != 2) {
        printf("Key not provided\n");
        return 1;
    }

    if (!strcmp(argv[1], "VTX")) {
        printf("KEY FOUND\n");
    } else {
        printf("Incorrect key: %s\n", argv[1]);
    }
    return 0;
}
```

### Expected behavior

I understand this isn't a bug as much as it's a factor of `time_per_instruction * num_instructions_before_main`. Is there something I am missing with regards to this sort of work flow which would allow a more rapid dev <-> test cycle? 

Issues https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/issues/70 and https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/issues/415 appear related.

issue_title: When compiling on CUDA, unnecessary compute architectures are generated
issue_body:
On this line, we are specifying that CUDA should compile on a bunch of different computation architectures from `compute_30` co `compute_52`:
https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/dynet/CMakeLists.txt#L125

In most cases, we probably only need to compile on a single computing architecture, the most recent one supported by the `nvcc` compiler and GPU. Because compiling for different architectures takes a long time and is a significant time bottleneck in getting DyNet installed on GPU, it would be nice to fix this. I'm not sure how to do so though, so if someone knows how help would be appreciated.

issue_title: operation: pow()
issue_body:
### In document:
![screen shot 2018-03-13 at 2 21 30 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/21228317/37361727-f5e2f8ae-26c9-11e8-9734-4a74674f25d3.png)

### However
```
t = dy.inputVector([1,2,3])
l = dy.pow(t, dy.scalarInput(2)) # works >> [1.0, 4.0, 9.0]
I = dy.pow(t, dy.inputVector([2]) # works >> [1.0, 4.0, 9.0]
l = dy.pow(t, dy.inputVector([2, 2, 2]))  # fails (1)
l = dy.pow(t, 2) # fails (2)
I = t ** 2 # fails (3)
```
### Failures
(1) The first failure contradicts the documents. 
It reports
![screen shot 2018-03-13 at 2 27 52 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/21228317/37362123-d23613ea-26ca-11e8-8992-915f6be301ad.png)
because of
![screen shot 2018-03-13 at 2 28 58 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/21228317/37362177-ec08dd8e-26ca-11e8-8035-5ebb9470c79e.png)
It **REQUIRES** second parameter as a 1-dim vector or scalar. **So this part of document is incorrect.**

(2, 3) Not a bug. (2) is only because the second parameter is required as an `Expression`; (3) without definition of reloading. It could possibly be more intuitive like `Expression**real` or `Expression**Expression` for python layer.

issue_title: Travis CI is slow, move to container based infrastructure on Travis
issue_body: Travis CI is becomes quite slow to start, moving to [Travis CI's container-based infrastructure](http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/workers/container-based-infrastructure/) should speed things up and may allow for caching of dependencies.  The difficulty is removing sudo commands, which are currently limited to apt-get.  The [apt addon](http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/workers/container-based-infrastructure/) can be used to solve this for gfortran but libglpk-dev is not white-listed and will break if/when Travis moves to a more recent version of Ubunutu with a version of GLPK which is not supported by pyglpk (4.53+).  Building a glpk and new pygkpk conda packages should be possible and would solve this block in addition to allowing easier installs of older and pyglpk compatible version of this library on modern Linux systems.

issue_title: Add possibility to restrict IPs by the IP's country
test if (or how much) the stoichiometric matrix is ill-conditioned
issue_body:
As pointed out by Steffen Klamt in the google document:

> "I find always useful is the absolute maximum and the absolute (non-zero) minimum of coefficients in the stoichiometric matrix since they tell us something about matrix conditioning and may point to potential numerical issues."

I think this is a very relevant point. I have also often encountered problems with numerical issues that were associated with ill-conditioned matrices (most times associated with the biomass coefficients). 

The condition number can be calculated in different ways:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condition_number

For this particular purpose, I think the absolute ratio between the largest and smallest coefficients should be sufficient. 

For instance, for iJO1366 the result is 2.7e7. From my (limited) experience, something above 1e9 should raise some flags...

issue_title: Fix Storage naming to (mostly) match API Schema
issue_body:
- [ ] Renaming noun "key" to "object" or close relative (#560).
- [ ] Remove `boto` aliases
- [ ] Address other concerning naming conventions

(Above added by @dhermes on 1/19/2014)

----

The naming conventions appear to be modeled around s3's boto library, but the library is not compatible with S3. It seems like it might be more useful to use the JSON API resource names. 
The major difference here is the use of "key" to represent an "object" resource.  The Cloud Storage API JSON docs typically use the word "key" to refer to an API key or a service account private key file, never to a cloud object resource.  Of course "object" is a reserved name in Python, but there are other possibilities.
With the hierarchy of connection/bucket/key, this looks a lot like boto without actually supporting s3, which could confuse users.

/cc @thobrla

issue_title: Adding fluentd hander
issue_body:
This is pretty overdue but one of the transports for the logging handler should be to write to a file that the google-fluentd agent knows how to pickup. This is how we recommend using logging on GAE Flex/GKE. [This handler](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/python-compat-runtime/blob/master/appengine-vmruntime/vmruntime/cloud_logging.py) is from the compat runtime and is roughly what we want to copy paste in here. 

I've realized I've made a slight design mistake, since the handler we wants inherits from `logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler`, but I already created a Handler class that inherits from `logging.handlers.StreamHandler` and I'm splitting the different ways to send to the API by a Transport class that the Handler uses.

I think it's still important that users can write a few lines of code, and have it do something reasonable both locally and on GAE Flex (i.e. locally write using the API transport, on Flex write to fluentd).

So alternatives are:
- Just break the current API and make a factory return different types of handlers instead of 1 handler with different types of transports. So instead of:

```

        import google.cloud.logging
        from google.cloud.logging.handlers import CloudLoggingHandler

        client = google.cloud.logging.Client()
        handler = CloudLoggingHandler(client)
```

something like 

```

        import google.cloud.logging
        from google.cloud.logging.handlers import get_logging_handler

        client = google.cloud.logging.Client()
        handler = get_logging_handler(client)
```

Then you can dynamically return the right type of handler.
- Use metaclasses somehow to do the above without breaking the current API (think this could be done but I know metaclasses are generally best avoided)
- Handroll my own file rotation code. Besides it being unnecessary code, it would also be nice if we kept the handler as close as possible to the compat one above.

I am thinking the first option might be best, since all this code is still pretty immature and it's better to just get things right/clean instead of maintaining perfect compatibility.  Thoughts? @jonparrott @dhermes @tseaver

issue_title: Remove datastore.key._dataset_ids_equal and just use string equality check
issue_body:
See #528 for some history here.

The issue is that requests made using `foo` instead of `s~foo` fail in [strange ways](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-datastore/issues/59).

So as a result we have to use `datastore.helpers._prepare_key_for_request` to strip dataset IDs from keys that get sent to the server. This has accidental caused bugs in the past (e.g. allowing keys from a different dataset `bar` to be inserted into dataset `foo`)

issue_title: DNS issue
issue_body:
在内网一台 debian 启了 ss python 版的服务，外网客户端通过公网动态域名来连接，没有问题。

内网有自架的dns服务，并将公网动态域名指向ss服务端内网的ip，ss服务端机器和客户端机器都是以它作为第一条 nameserver，之后是两条 opendns和两条googledns。

当内网ss客户端（python版 sslocal）启动后会在服务端日志里留下一条正确的客户端内网ip，之后都会变成“from 192.168.1.1”， 也就是说客户端除了第一次解析到服务端内网地址外，之后都解析到了公网地址。

之后也分别测过将已知的域名比如 example.com 和主机名 serverA 也在dns服务中指向服务端内网ip，前者在服务端日志留下一条地址正确的记录后就不能再连了，后者都是正确的连接状态。

于是现在暂时是简单粗暴的在 asyncdns.py#L302 后面加了个break。

不清楚是bug还是特意这么设计的，另外如果有描述不到位的地方，还请多包涵，谢谢。

issue_title: Pants should support Node.js source-level dependencies
issue_body:
# Summary
Pants is optimized to support developing against large-scale, multi-project codebase with complex dependencies on local and third party libraries. Node.js should support source-level dependencies similar to jvm, python, go, etc...

# Proposal
There are two parts that would need to change in Pants to make this happen.

- First, there should be a new **install** goal that would replace the developer using the system Node.js package manager for preparing their workspace. Internally, the **install** task workflow will be reused for resolving node_module dependencies.
- Secondly, there should be a way to publish node_modules that includes source-level dependencies.

# Design

### Install
- Source level dependencies modules should be listed as `file://` with relative path in `package.json`.
Each dependency should have a default Pants build target that is the node_module target, i.e. use the directory name as the target name. This will enforce the **1:1:1 rule**.
- During codegen step, parse `package.json` and translate `file://` into Pants target.  Those targets will be inserted into Pants BuildGraph. 
- During resolution phase, all third party packages will be installed first, then symbolic links under node_modules directory will be created to link to source dependencies. This new symlinking process is not part of the standard build process.
- Node_module target will have a new build attribute `node_scope` which will scope the symlink dir under `node_module/@<node_scope>/project`. If a project name already has scope defined in the package.json, the node_scope will be used instead. If no node_scope is defined, business as usual.
- During build phase, each source dependency will be built first (specified by `build_script` field), the output results of the build process will be used for linking, then the dependent module will be built.  Note this is different behavior of using npm to install since `file://` dependencies translate to link action, and no lifecycle scripts will be invoked. 
- If there are any source-dependencies, the **installation** will need to be forced to pick up any source changes. 
- No other lifecycle scripts will be invoked. This is an existing condition.


### Publish

- A new field will be added to the node_module target `shared_module_path` which will default to shared_module_path.
- A new field will be added to the node_module target `node_publish_script`.
- A new option will be added to the node subsystem options with an incremental target-level option, `node_ignore_publish_regex_paths` that specifies which file path patterns to ignore when copying during the publish step.
- Prior to the build phase, if there is a source dependency that specifies a different package manager as the root, fail with error message. All source-dependencies must be built within the same package manager.
- During the build phase, each source dependency will be copied over (ignoring paths from `node_ignore_publish_regex_paths`) to the `shared_module_path` with the directory format `shared_module_path/@scope/project_name`.
- The package.json name will be updated to match the correct “scope” and the `file://` path to reflect the new copied path. 
- Publish will be invoked from the underlying package manager following the same **@scope** inheritance rules as defined in previous steps. All lifecycle scripts will be invoked per package manager rules.
- If a `node_prepublish_script` is specified, this will be ran as well. (It is recommended to use this script rather than the supported package_manager’s lifecycle scripts).
- Publishing a top-level node_module package will not publish any source-dependency node_module package.

# Drawback
The most relevant drawback is the replacement of `./pants install` over the native package_manager install. Currently, node_module targets only support the “build” lifecycle script and nothing else.

Publishing only publishes the root target and transitive source-deps are not published. The published package is comparably statically linked rather than a dynamically linked.

# Other Discussions

### How about Yarn Workspaces
Yarn workspaces allow node projects to depend on another local project. Yarn workspaces was officially released in version 1.x (Yarn 1.7+ introduces focused workspaces that allows you to install local dependencies through the registry that can speed up smaller changes). In Yarn workspaces, all local projects are specified in a root package.json and typically live under a shared directory. When a project adds a local dependency, the dependency is symlinked from the shared directory into the project’s node_modules.

To summarize some Yarn workspaces limitations:

- Requires a root package.json (Cannot express complex code organization, all targets/projects need to live under this package.json)
- Installing a project will require rebuilding all local dependencies (focused workspaces rely on packaged versions)
- Workspaces don’t run lifecycle scripts for packages not defined at the root package.json. See [3911](https://github.com/yarnpkg/yarn/issues/3911).
- Doesn’t follow the **1:1:1 rule**.

### Yarn vs NPM

The discussion about making a choice whether or not to support a single package manager has come up more than once offline. With some historical context, Yarn was released with all intentions to be as cross-compatible with NPM as possible. I believe this to reduce the complexity to migrate from NPM to Yarn. With introduction of experimental workspaces in Yarn 0.27.5, Yarn has been pushing the differences between NPM and Yarn. There are many minute differences across NPM and Yarn and I anticipate that in future’s time, the landscape between the two package manager may be mutually exclusive. This increases the maintenance burden on pants compatibility across the two tools. For reference internally at Twitter, I believe there to be 4x more Yarn projects than npm projects currently using the Pants ecosystem. If there is an argument for supporting a single package manager, the most obvious solution would be to support both package manager and new feature development be added to the “supported” package manager and have some deprecation commitment on the previous package manager.

issue_title: example: java/hello's 3rdparty dep isn't in org/junit subdir
issue_body:
Our tutorial uses junit as its example 3rdparty dep.

For workspaces w/a ton of 3rdparty deps, we recommend the targets live in 3rdparty/jvm/com/sunorwhatever/BUILD rather than 3rdparty/BUILD .

The Pants repo itself doesn't follow this rule. That's OK, we're not likely to accumulate a ton of 3rdparty JVM deps. *knocks wood to avoid jinx*

So our example code ain't exemplary; folks who learn by glancing at sample code instead of slogging through Best Practices will lump all their jar_libs in one file; later, they'll regret it.

issue_title: Bundle behaviour change for directories in v2
issue_body:
The `v2` engine currently captures matched directories in `Snapshot`s, but doesn't actually propagate them through to `FilesetWithSpec`. One effect of this is the `jvm_app` `bundles` that explicitly refer to directories don't actually end up recursively including the directory content.

The `fileset` args here are matching directories, rather than files, and the semantics of `fileset` are ill defined. The following bundle fileset:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/')
```
...will not match any files in v2. But changing the glob to:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/**/*')
```
...will recursively match all files under the directory, similar to the v1 engine.

----

I think there are two changes to make here.

1. We should include all matched paths in the FilesetWithSpec in order to support the existing bundle case, and because it is completely reasonable for `tasks`/`Rules` to be able to capture directories.
2. We should add a deprecation warning for bundles for the case where a bundle matches _only_  directories. The idea is not to ban matching directories in the long term (that's totally reasonable)... instead, the goal would be to try to detect cases where people were expecting everything under a directory to be recursively included by a `fileset`, and instead ask them to change to an explicit recursive glob if that's what they wanted.

issue_body:

```

So I did a spot check. There is a processor for com.squareup.service.container.Application, and it is configured.

```
$ ./pants depmap <target>
...

                internal-service.container.annotation-processors.src.main.java.app-config-processor
...
```

I see that with JMake and the twitter commons Compiler, there is a `-Tnowarnregex` option that was suppressing these before.   @stuhood reports that Twitter filed an internal bug for it.

Consensus in the group discussion is that we should do something similar in zinc that we did with jmake.  Maybe this time build something into pants to ignore certain error message output instead of passing an argument to zinc.

issue_title: Allow python projects to pull dependencies from VCS
issue_body:
Per pants documentation:
> python_requirements defines a named target for each line in the requirements.txt line. For example, a line like ansicolors==1.0.2 in requirements.txt defines a target named ansicolors that pulls in ansicolors version 1.0.2.

The python requirements.txt file format allows packages to be installed in an editable form, which 

>  puts the source code into src/distname (making the name lower case) and runs python setup.py develop on the package. To indicate editable, use -e, like:
-e svn+http://svn.myproject.org/svn/MyProject/trunk#egg=MyProject

https://pip.readthedocs.org/en/1.1/requirements.html#the-requirements-file-format

This feature supported by requirements.txt allows many python projects to grab dependencies from GitHub or other source code repositories. The fact that pants doesn't support this means dependencies not published on the Python Package Index need to be copied into individual repos and defined as a python library. It'd be great if pants could allow specifying these kind of dependencies in the requirements.txt file.

issue_title: Replace `pytest_run.py` use of `--resultlog` with `--junitxml`.
issue_body:
In order to upgrade pytest, coverage et. al. we need to replace the current use of `--resultlog` with something else since its deprecated and slated for removal.  The py.test 3.0.2 output is:

```
...
platform linux2 -- Python 2.7.12, pytest-3.0.2, py-1.4.31, pluggy-0.3.1
rootdir: /, inifile: 
plugins: timeout-1.0.0, cov-2.3.1
collected 12 items
...
============= pytest-warning summary =============
...
WC1 None --result-log is deprecated and scheduled for removal in pytest 4.0
...
```

We already parse junit xml format in `junit_run.py` to find the same data, and since `py.test` and `pytest_run.py` already support this format, it seems reasonable to switch to parsing it.

issue_title: register use-nailgun as a global option so override config is less verbose
issue_body:
Running pants in our Java based CI environment in mesos is constrained by a fixed pre-allocated memory. One of the memory variables is the number NG instances.

To restrict the number of NG used and because *use-nailgun* has a default value *True*, we currently turn the setting off task by task in the override config, and it becomes quite verbose. Like this

> [task1]
> use_nailgun: False
> [task2]
> use_nailgun: True
> ...
> [taskn]
> use_nailgun: False

Not to mention we also have to take care adding new tasks.

We do leave NG on for a few tasks like *thrift-linter*, *compile.scala* that bring the most benefits. An override config setting like below would be more readable and less error prone

> [DEFAULT]
> use_nailgun: False
> [thrift-linter]
> use_nailgun: True
> [compile.scala]
> use_nailgun: True

issue_title: ^C kills the shell, not the command
issue_body: I may wish to cancel a command that's either not responding or which I realize is going to take too long (or for any number of other reasons), such as "ec2 describe-instances" with no filters, or "s3 ls <path>" with a prefix that contains millions of files.  The natural reflex in this situation is to hit ^C, which I was surprised to discover kills the whole shell.  Exiting the shell is what I expect ^D to do (and indeed that works).  I expect the code is simply not catching KeyboardInterrupt and/or setting a handler for SIGINT...

The Lambdas have an IAM Role that includes AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole so SAM got that part right.  The SAM missed something that the manual deploy and configuration got right.

issue_title: Orphan wal-prefetch processes hog all system resources
issue_body:
Not sure if this might happen with other backends, but at least happens with GCS:

While running the current master branch with python 3.4 I'm left with an ever growing number of wal-prefetch processes that never die. This will finally cause the OOM killer to kick in.

All of the zombies look like this:
```
postgres 124520  Sl   05:38   0:00 python bin/wal-e wal-prefetch /var/lib/postgresql/9.5/main/pg_xlog 000000010000096E0000007F
postgres 124561  SL   05:38   0:00  \_ gpg -d -q --batch
postgres 124562  Z    05:38   0:00  \_ [lzop] <defunct>
```

From the logs it looks like a wal-fetch is called for the same segment shortly after the wal-prefetch starts. There is nothing in the logs that would indicate what went wrong with the zombie wal-prefetch process.

issue_title: How to clear a particular metric's all labels and values
issue_body:
According to the prometheus documentation in the below link,
https://prometheus.io/docs/instrumenting/writing_clientlibs/#labels

Metrics with labels SHOULD support a remove() method with the same signature as labels() that will remove a Child from the metric no longer exporting it, **and a clear() method that removes all Children from the metric. These invalidate caching of Children.**

This seems to be missing in this python library.

my_gauge_metric = Gauge(..)

my_gauage_metric._metrics.clear() would do the job, but I felt little discomfort in using underscore variables outside. Also, its not thread safe since i should have used self._lock

Would be great if this is fixed in the new updates of this library.

Clearing of a particular metric is needed in Guage since the python layer will constatntly give the old Guage values even though its not generated in the latest time.
