issue_title: font.highlight_color Not Working With Styles
issue_body:
`font.highlight_color` appears to work fine when applied directly to a run's font, this way.

```
run=para.add_run("this should have a yellow background, but with no style (!)")
run.font.highlight_color=docx.enum.text.WD_COLOR.YELLOW
```

When applied using a style, it seems to have no effect.

```
style=doc.styles.add_style("highlightYellow",docx.enum.style.WD_STYLE_TYPE.CHARACTER)
style.base_style=doc.styles["Normal"]
style.font.highlight_color=docx.enum.text.WD_COLOR.YELLOW

para.add_run("this should have a yellow background, but does not",style)
```

In Microsoft Word, I can see that the style is being applied to that run, but it's background isn't yellow.  If I look at the style using the _Modify Style_ dialog, the sample text snippet shows a yellow background, oddly enough.  But in that dialog, _Format/Border.../Shading_ says that _Fill_ is _No Color_.  Selecting yellow there fixes it.  Another oddity is that I do get a yellow background opening the same document in LibreOffice, oowriter.

I'm gathering some XML to help isolate this, and will attach it to the issue.

issue_title: Traffic model bug? "optimistic" slower than "best-guess"
issue_body:
Hello, I found that traffic_model="optimistic" sometimes takes longer than traffic_model="best guess". I don't know if this is because of the library or because of Google Maps, but I thought I'd ask, because it makes me question the results using "optimistic" more generally. See attached example, and example Python code:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6221182/11506788/1c8f5420-981f-11e5-826d-71fc1bceec64.png)

Code output:
['Travel time from: 12.979572,80.25271 to 12.921726,80.230478']
['Departure time (local): 16 Dec 2015 03:42:02']
['Time, no traffic, minutes: 12.566666666666666']
['Time in traffic, minutes: 9.45']
['Time in traffic (optimistic), minutes: 10.45']

Code:
__author__ = 'Gabriel Kreindler, gek@mit.edu'

'''
This code shows that the traffic_model option sometimes generates counter-intuitive results.
The option traffic_model="optimistic" sometimes generates longer travel times compared to "best_guess" or "pessimistic".
(The latter is not shown here.)
'''

import googlemaps
import time
import calendar

# client
client = googlemaps.Client(key='KEY HERE')

# origin and destination
orig="12.979572,80.25271"
dest="12.921726,80.230478"

# query time
t1 = time.strptime("15 Dec 2015 22:12:02", "%d %b %Y %H:%M:%S")
nsec1 = calendar.timegm(t1)

# get offset
timezone_offset = client.timezone(orig, timestamp=nsec1)
timezone_offset_sec = timezone_offset['rawOffset']

# nice local time
t1_loc = time.gmtime(nsec1 + timezone_offset_sec) # local time
dep_time_local = time.strftime("%d %b %Y %H:%M:%S", t1_loc)


# get duration in traffic
dist = client.distance_matrix(orig, dest, departure_time=nsec1, mode="driving")

# parse
temp = dist["rows"][0]["elements"][0]
assert temp["status"]=="OK"
dist_output = temp["distance"]["value"]
dur_output = temp["duration"]["value"]  # duration
dur_intraffic_output = temp["duration_in_traffic"]["value"]

# get duration in traffic
dist = client.distance_matrix(orig, dest, departure_time=nsec1, mode="driving", traffic_model="optimistic")

# parse
temp = dist["rows"][0]["elements"][0]
assert temp["status"]=="OK"
dur_intraffic_opt_output = temp["duration_in_traffic"]["value"]

print(['Travel time from: ' + orig + ' to ' + dest])
print(['Departure time (local): ' + dep_time_local])
print(['Time, no traffic, minutes: ' + str(dur_output/60)])
print(['Time in traffic, minutes: ' + str(dur_intraffic_output/60)])
print(['Time in traffic (optimistic), minutes: ' + str(dur_intraffic_opt_output/60)])

issue_title: Context Processor Tests should test the effect of the context processor on a context, not do a request against the server
issue_body:
Currently our Context Processor Tests take the following form:
```
from __future__ import absolute_import, print_function, unicode_literals

import json

from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse
from rest_framework.test import APITestCase

from kolibri.auth.models import Facility, DeviceOwner, FacilityUser


class ContextProcessorTestCase(APITestCase):

    def test_context_processor_anonymous_user(self):
        response = self.client.get(reverse('kolibri:setupwizardplugin:setupwizard'))
        self.assertEqual(json.loads(response.context['session'])['kind'], ['ANONYMOUS'])

    def test_context_processor_user(self):
        self.facility = Facility.objects.create(name="QQQ")
        DeviceOwner.objects.create(username="admin", password="***")
        self.user = FacilityUser.objects.create(username="foo", facility=self.facility)
        self.user.set_password("bar")
        self.user.save()
        self.client.login(username="foo", password="bar")
        response = self.client.get(reverse('kolibri:learnplugin:learn'))
        self.assertEqual(json.loads(response.context['session'])['username'], "foo")
```

However, these are not unit tests of the context processor, and come much closer to an integration test, for which we need a full frontend build. I am deleting these in #546, but they should be replaced by proper unit tests.

issue_title: Plugins dependencies and build configuration have to be present in the top level configuration files
issue_body:
At the moment, our plugins pollute the global configuration for their npm dependencies and specific webpack configuration needs.

This is unnecessary as each one is built separately, so they do not have to share anything but the base configuration.

Solution: Each plugin can (optionally) have its own package.json, and its own webpack config.

We can use https://www.npmjs.com/package/recursive-install to recursively install dependencies inside each plugin, and inject the individual webpack configurations into the overall configuration for that bundle at build time.

issue_body:
It will be fairly common for a previously accessible Kolibri instance to become unavailable.

Some examples:

* a student may be using a tablet and walk out of range of a hotspot
* a laptop hosting kolibri may run out of battery power
* someone the host machine remains accessible, but someone shuts down the kolibri instance running on it

We should look at both the technical and UX side of things.

1. Write code to catch and handle errors these kinds of errors holistically, and set some global vuex state that represents whether the host is accessible
2. Add a pop-up, overlay, or similar to indicate to the user that the app is disconnected. Potentially prevent further interaction with the app until it reconnects.  (cc @jtamiace @khangmach)

We probably don't want to go down the 'leave parts usable when disconnected' rabbit hole yet...

issue_title: Make the code once again python 2.7 compatible
issue_body:
There are various options for speeding up our code. The most obvious is to use pypy, but numpy isn't available under python 3 (which I ported us to earlier in the year). It would be good to still be Python 2 compatible, so I'm going to look at how much work that is. Not too much I don't think.

We still wont be able to use pypy though, as it doesn't know what to do with the GOR IV C code. But we can make that run under `cffi` and then we should be good to go.

my intention is to integrate exabgp into my own python program. So i do not want to make use of the process functionality. I integrate it as subprocess:
```

                                ,'resources/exabgp_configuration.ini'],
                                stdout=subprocess.PIPE,

This makes me able to read from exabps stdout. My initial start for testing was to instruct a Thread to continiously search for Updates in stdout by using this function:
```
    def exabgp_output_parser(self):
        while True:
            try:

issue_title: Use zero copy ffi.from_buffer for bytes types in `Socket.send()`
issue_body:
With respect to `Socket.send()`...

In cffi 1.8 you can use ffi.from_buffer on bytes types which provides a nice perf boost over ffi.new() when the data is large. (30% in my tests for a ~1MB buffer).

It also might also be more efficient to avoid the type checking and just assume a from_buffer type will be used (fast path) and do any conversion to a bytes type in an exception handler (slow path).

I've not tested on python2 nor do I understand the ramifications of such a change to that platform.

issue_title: Bug in cardinalities dict in MarkovModel
issue_body: ```python >>> graph = MarkovModel() >>> graph.add_edges_from([('a', 'b'), ('b', 'c'), ('c', 'd'),('d', 'a')]) >>> phi1 = Factor(['a', 'b'], [4, 4], np.random.rand(16)) >>> graph.add_factors(phi1) >>> graph.cardinalities defaultdict(, {}) >>> graph.check_model() True >>> graph.cardinalities defaultdict(, {'a': 4, 'b': 4}) >>> graph.remove_factors(phi1) >>> graph.cardinalities defaultdict(, {'a': 4, 'b': 4}) >>> phi2 = Factor(['a', 'b'], [3, 4], np.random.rand(12)) >>> graph.add_factors(phi2) >>> graph.cardinalities defaultdict(, {'a': 4, 'b': 4}) >>> graph.check_model() Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in graph.check_model() File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\pgmpy-0.1.0-py3.4.egg\pgmpy\models\MarkovModel.py", line 199, in check_model 'Cardinality of variable %s not matching among factors' % variable) pgmpy.exceptions.Exceptions.CardinalityError: 'Cardinality of variable a not matching among factors' ``` The cardinalities dict should be updated with add_factors and remove_factors method and not with check_model method. In the above example check_model should not throw VaueError after removing phi1 and then adding phi2.

issue_title: solve intersecting outline paths in font instances
issue_body:
notolints report a lot of intersecting outline paths in ttf files generated from sources in https://github.com/googlei18n/noto-source/tree/master/src. 

For example, 

Automatic testing for 'NotoSansArabic-Black.ttf', Version 1.902:
E <paths/intersection> The glyph 'dotlessbeh.ar' has intersecting outlines: intersection ((478.0, -21.0), (504.0, 147.0)) and ((478.0, -21.0), (310.0, -21.0), (219.0, 13.5))

![unnamed](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/12721521/20997690/dcab241a-bcbc-11e6-9911-094f5b03c517.jpg)

It'll be great if fontmake can solve the intersecting outline paths.

@jamesgk @dougfelt @marekjez86

issue_title: Warnings returned by the driver should not raise
issue_body:
If the underlying driver runtime returns a warning, as it stands today, the Python binding raises.
One example of this is when a DMM reads over-range. It is undesirable to change flow control of the program in these scenarios.

[nidaqmx](https://github.com/ni/nidaqmx-python) handles warnings by logging them to file. We should look into their approach and deal with warnings in the same manner.

We should also log when driver's close function errors.

issue_title: niswitch review notes
issue_body:
# Description of issue

We reviewed the niswitch module with NI-SWITCH domain experts. We have a few action items prior version 1.0.

Remove the following attributes that apply only to obsolete hardware

* cabled_module_scan_advanced_bus = _attributes.AttributeViInt32(1150009)
* cabled_module_trigger_bus = _attributes.AttributeViInt32(1150008)
* master_slave_scan_advanced_bus = _attributes.AttributeViInt32(1150007)
* master_slave_trigger_bus = _attributes.AttributeViInt32(1150006)
* parsed_scan_list = _attributes.AttributeViString(1150012)
* trigger_mode = _attributes.AttributeViInt32(1150005)

Remove the following methods, since they have corresponding properties

* configure_scan_list(self, scanlist, scan_mode=enums.ScanMode.BREAK_BEFORE_MAKE)
* configure_scan_trigger(self, trigger_input, scan_advanced_output, scan_delay=datetime.timedelta(seconds=0.0))
* route_scan_advanced_output(self, scan_advanced_output_connector, scan_advanced_output_bus_line, invert=False)
* route_trigger_input(self, trigger_input_connector, trigger_input_bus_line, invert=False)
* set_continuous_scan(self, continuous_scan)

Be consistent about trigger polarity. Did we remove in `nifgen` and `nidcpower`?
* scan_advanced_polarity = _attributes.AttributeEnum(_attributes.AttributeViInt32, enums.ScanAdvancedPolarity, 1150011)

issue_title: Expose payload transparency for user code
issue_body:
"payload transparency" is a feature of WAMP AP. Please see [here](https://github.com/wamp-proto/wamp-proto/issues/228). In particular the use cases described there.

This feature is implemented in AutobahnPython and Crossbar.io, but is currently NOT exposed at the user API level in any direct way.

It is only used _internally_ to implement WAMP-cryptobox. And the latter feature is only experimental/alpha. So we can change things.

As from recent discussion on IRC (#autobahn), there is strong interest in letting user code hook into the "payload transparency" feature.

FWIW, and for the record, I was long fighting for NOT exposing this to user code. But I changed my mind;) Well, the accumulated feedback and arguments from multiple users finally convinced me that the required changes, introduction of new API and deviation from a WAMP principle (automatic payload conversion / serializer independence) are worth it. And that users should be free choose their tradeoff on their requirements - not us. We can't make the tradeoffs involved magically disappear, but I now think we can expose it in a way that still fits into WAMP and keeps the coherence intact.

issue_title: Test fixtures should be built locally with the git-asdf1234 tags
issue_body:
Currently, `test-postgresql` and `test-etcd` are built as an automated build on Docker Hub. `mock-store` is built and pushed manually.

In #1351, we added an optimization to only pull these images if they don't already exist. For changes to `test-postgresql` or `test-etcd`, this means that we'll continue to use the old images until someone manually nukes the images on the test boxes. 

All three of these test fixtures should be refactored to:
- Not push to Docker Hub (disable automated builds)
- Build locally with the same `git-asdf1234` hash as the rest of the components
- Use the `git-asdf1234` image name in test runs, ensuring we always use the latest version

issue_title: Use
Proposal: disable /router-nginx-status endpoint in production
issue_body:
Currently, the default virtual host on `deis-router` exposes a `/router-nginx-status` endpoint.

If a request is made that hits the router and isn't matched by the virtual host for any specific app, it will be served by the default virtual host.

A cleverly constructed URL like the one below allows visibility into the router from the outside world.

Example:

http://any-subdomain-that-is-not-an-app.krancour.deis.ninja/router-nginx-status

For fear that this information could be exploited, I believe we should add the capability to disable this endpoint in production.  Better yet, IMO, we should disable it by default and allow operators who understand the risk to opt-in.

This is on Deis 1.9 and CoreOS 647.2.0

When trying to clone a public repository via SSH during deployment (using buildpacks), we get a permission denied error.  I can reproduce this error by jumping into the builder container and running the git clone command manually:

```
bash-4.3# git clone git@github.com:babel/broccoli-babel-transpiler.git
Cloning into 'broccoli-babel-transpiler'...
Warning: Permanently added the RSA host key for IP address '192.30.252.130' to the list of known hosts.
Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.

Please make sure you have the correct access rights
and the repository exists.
```

The same command works fine locally and on the CoreOS host itself:

```
/tmp $ git clone https://github.com/babel/broccoli-babel-transpiler.git
Cloning into 'broccoli-babel-transpiler'...
remote: Counting objects: 403, done.
remote: Total 403 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 403
Receiving objects: 100% (403/403), 60.93 KiB | 0 bytes/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (225/225), done.
Checking connectivity... done.
```

Interestingly enough, using https inside the builder seems to work fine so it seems that there may be an issue with SSH inside the container:

```
bash-4.3# git clone https://github.com/babel/broccoli-babel-transpiler.git
Cloning into 'broccoli-babel-transpiler'...
remote: Counting objects: 403, done.        
remote: Total 403 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 403        
Receiving objects: 100% (403/403), 60.93 KiB | 0 bytes/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (225/225), done.
Checking connectivity... done.
```

Thanks

issue_title: deisctl: Add 'backup' and 'restore' commands
issue_body: We have the [backup/restore](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/backing_up_data/) docs, but `deisctl` itself should have a backup/restore command to ease this process. We could also back up and restore etcd keys, which is not something we currently do - we just repopulate them from the database.

issue_title: Make the default plotted graph exportable by default
issue_body:
+ @thibaudh @briantrice @padentomasello @int3h 

When plotting, we should make the default 'more friendly' for exporting as HTML. For example, imagine laying out for a few seconds & auto-saving, and the next time the same URL is loaded, start "offline" from that point. Likewise, an in-visualization save (new camera angle, added filters, ...) would update that visualization. The result is that, when I'm "done" with a notebook, I can just hit "export as html" without having to do anything special and it'll "just work".

Does this seem feasible, ideas on ~workarounds, ...? We may need StreamGL to cooperate a bit and be a bit smarter, but similar to the auto-splashscreen & play, seems doable.

Benefits:

* Notebooks are faster to rerun

issue_title: Confusion about cursor.commit()
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is a bug, oversight, or something I'm doing wrong. 

From the documentation [here](http://turbodbc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/advanced_usage.html#autocommit) it looks to me like I'm supposed to be able to call `cursor.commit()` to commit changes. However, I always get the error:

```
    cursor.commit()
AttributeError: 'Cursor' object has no attribute 'commit'
```
The main [transaction documentation](http://turbodbc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/getting_started.html#transactions) only has documenation using the connection. 
 
Looking at https://github.com/blue-yonder/turbodbc/blob/master/python/turbodbc/cursor.py I also don't see a commit method. Am I missing something?

issue_title: Add simple instructions for dependencies, build, and install
issue_body:
There is no top-level document (e.g. the Read Me) stating how to satisfy dependencies, build, and install this code base.

There are CMake files, a Distutils script, a Vagrantfile, a Makefile. Which of those should the recipient use to satisfy build dependencies? What commands to run for building, for installing?

A simple text in the Read Me document (or, if they are more complex, maybe a separate INSTALL document) that says something like:

    Get build dependencies:

        $ python3 -m pip install foo bar baz

    Build this code base:

        $ cmake lorem/
        $ python3 -m setup build

    Install the library:

        $ make install

would be enough to satisfy the recipient's need to know how to do those things.

issue_title: Use `NamedTuple` instead of `Dict` for Command messages
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

We currently are using dictionaries for the `msg` portion for commands like `GetBlockHeaders`.  It's hard to specify types for these, as well as them being inherently mutable structures.

### How can it be fixed

Commands should decode into a `NamedTuple` instead when they have key/value data.

issue_title: Add retry middleware
issue_body:
### What was wrong?
It's not uncommon for requests sent over the network to fail due to network interruptions, and in most cases retrying the request will resolve the issue.

### How can it be fixed?
Implement simple retry middleware to handle retry on HTTP errors automatically.

issue_title: Add a replace_exceptions decorator
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Exception replacement is a common need when stitching together a lot of libraries.

Web3.py and eth-tester can both use it, at least.

### How can it be fixed?

Add a `replace_exceptions` implementation, like the one in eth-tester:

https://github.com/ethereum/eth-tester/commit/9f92225bf1a2155ce7ebb5a0dc22ed3ab4895b5e

issue_title: deactivate bug
conda create --clone creates broken env when crossing filesystems
issue_body:
The conda create subcommand attempts to create hard links from source (non-symlink) files in the cloned env to the destination env.  However, when source and destination directories are on different filesystems, most commonly in an NFS environment, creation of those hard links fails.  The resulting environment has many broken links to shared libraries, as the actual shared library contents never get copied.

Creation of the hard links is attempted in conda.install._link when link type is LINK_HARD.  The underlying call to os.link throws an exception, but the exception is caught by the install.link() function and logged somewhere.  No error message gets written to the console.

The use of the --copy option in conda create does not change the behavior: hard links are still attempted, but silently fail.

I suggest either (1) detecting cross-filesysystem clones and copy files in that case instead of trying to hard link them; or (2) make --copy change the behavior of conda.install._link to use LINK_COPY.

In addition, the catching of the OSError exception in conda.install.link seems misguided.  Failure to create a link means the created env is in all likelihood broken.  The install should just quit and report the error on the console, and ensure that the script exits nonzero.

issue_title: build environment shebang too long for Linux: "bad interpreter"
issue_body:
@janssen commented on [Wed Oct 12 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453)

I've got a build.sh which installs a tool, then calls it to install a couple of other things.  But the tool invocation fails:

```
/local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/work/conda_build.sh: /local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_/bin/garden: /local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/_b_env_placehold_placehold_pl: bad interpreter: No such file or directory
Command failed: /bin/bash -x -e /local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/work/conda_build.sh
```

If you look at the top of bin/garden, you'll find this shebang:

```
#!/local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_/bin/python
```

Now, that Python exists, and is executable.  And we know that the "bad interpreter" error refers to the shebang being too long.  It's about twice as long as the typical Linux max shebang length of 127.


---

@msarahan commented on [Thu Oct 13 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-253517289)

conda takes care of this on package install by setting up a shortcut of sorts.  If you're installing that script yourself in build.sh, you'll need to do something similar.

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/install.py#L281-L289


---

@janssen commented on [Thu Oct 13 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-253559603)

So the shebangs in the built packages are still bad?  Still seems like kind of a bug; shouldn't something like "#!/usr/bin/env python" be preferable?  I presume the whole chain of _placehold frobs are there to make enough space in DLLs to properly relocate the RPATH, but that line of reasoning doesn't really apply to shebangs.


---

@msarahan commented on [Thu Oct 13 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-253564950)

Shebangs in built packages are bad until conda installs them and applies the function I linked above.

The placehold stuff is indeed there to make enough space in executables and shared libraries.

Are you proposing that conda-build apply some kind of shebang fixing before packaging?  If so, when exactly?  Conda-build cedes control to the user during bld.bat/build.sh execution, and then immediately bundles up files.  Is there a place where some intermediate shebang fix would help?


---

@janssen commented on [Tue Oct 18 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-254662883)

Fair enough.


---

@jjhelmus commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264679973)

I've run into this issue while trying to rebuild some `conda-forge` packages with long prefixes.  Packages installed by conda-build into the build environment contain long shebang lines which cannot be executed in the environment.  Is there a work around for this?  Should conda-build be setting up shortcuts for these shebang lines?  

Specifically the example I've been debugging is building [`pkg-config`](https://github.com/conda-forge/pkg-config-feedstock).  This recipe installs the `autoconf` package in the build environment which includes the autom4te script.  This is a perl script with a shebang line in the environment of:

```
#! /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/pkg-config-feedstock_1480817967516/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold/bin/perl -w
```

When this script is executed via the execution of `aclocal` in by the `build.sh` script it fails due to the long shebang line.

---

@msarahan commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264682025)

As long as the scripts being run are actually parts of some other package, this should not be happening - things should get fixed by conda when that other package is installed into the build environment.

I think what is going wrong for you, @jjhelmus, is that the shebang line contains an unusual space separator on the left.  I don't think it's syntactically wrong, but it might throw off conda's machinery for shortening the hashbang.

---

@jjhelmus commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264682952)

I worked out what is happening to shebang lines but I'm not sure if the fault issue is with `conda` or `conda-build`.  

The issue lies in shebang lines which contain a shace between the `!` and the path to the interpreter.  For example:  `#! /usr/bin/perl`. These type of shebang lines are only correctly updated to a "shortcut" prefix if the executable contains the text `python`.  

This is best shown with the following pair of recipes which creates three shebang scripts (foo) and print out the contents of these files in the build environment (bar).

## foo recipe:

meta.yaml
```
package:
  name: foo
  version: 1.0
```

build.sh
```
#!/bin/sh

echo "#! ${PREFIX}/bin/python" > say_hi.py
echo "print('Hello')" >> say_hi.py
cat say_hi.py
cp say_hi.py $PREFIX/bin/

echo "#! ${PREFIX}/bin/perl" > say_hi.pl
echo "print('Hello')" >> say_hi.pl
cat say_hi.pl
cp say_hi.pl $PREFIX/bin/

echo "#! ${PREFIX}/bin/lala" > say_hi.la
echo "print('Hello')" >> say_hi.la
cat say_hi.la
cp say_hi.la $PREFIX/bin/
```

## bar recipe

meta.yaml
```
package:
  name: bar
  version: 1.0

requirements:
  build:
    - foo
  run:
    - foo
```

build.sh

```
#!/bin/sh

ls $PREFIX/bin

cat ${PREFIX}/bin/say_hi.py
cat ${PREFIX}/bin/say_hi.pl
cat ${PREFIX}/bin/say_hi.la

exit 1
```

With conda `4.2.13` and conda-build `2.0.11` building the foo package adds three files to the ./bin/ directory.  Examining the contents of these files shows that there is no space in the shebang line of the file with `python` in the executable path, due to the logic in the `fix_prefix` function in the `post.py` module of conda-build.  The other scripts retain the space in the shebang line.

```
~/.../test/foo$ tar xf ~/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64/foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2 bin/say_hi.py -O
#!/opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/bin/python
print('Hello')
~/.../test/foo$ tar xf ~/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64/foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2 bin/say_hi.pl -O
#! /opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/bin/perl
print('Hello')
~/.../test/foo$ tar xf ~/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64/foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2 bin/say_hi.la -O
#! /opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/bin/lala
print('Hello')
```

Now when these scripts are install into a conda environment with a long prefix as if done in conda build only the shebang line of the python script is rewritten as a shortcut `usr/bin/env` due to the SHEBANG_REGEX in the `install.py` module of `conda.

```
$ conda build .
BUILD START: bar-1.0-0
updating index in: /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64
updating index in: /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/noarch

The following packages will be downloaded:

    package                    |            build
    ---------------------------|-----------------
    foo-1.0                    |                0           2 KB  local

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    foo: 1.0-0 local

Package: bar-1.0-0
source tree in: /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/work
+ source /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/bin/activate /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_eh
+ ls /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placeholn
activate  conda  deactivate  say_hi.la	say_hi.pl  say_hi.py
+ cat /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehoy
#!/usr/bin/env python
print('Hello')
+ cat /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehol
#! /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_l
print('Hello')
+ cat /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehoa
#! /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_a
print('Hello')
+ exit 1
Command failed: /bin/bash -x -e /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/work/conda_build.sh
~/.../test/bar$ 
```

I can see logic in having `conda-build` always rewrite shebang lines without a space and a placeholder or having `conda` rewrite shebang lines which contain a space.  Both could also be implemented. Personally I would prefer the later is that (with a space) is how I write my shebang lines and is how they were [first written by Dennis Ritchie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shebang_(Unix)#History) 

Happy to open new issue(s) in either repository on this topic.

---

@jjhelmus commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264683083)

> I think what is going wrong for you, @jjhelmus, is that the shebang line contains an unusual space separator on the left. I don't think it's syntactically wrong, but it might throw off conda's machinery for shortening the hashbang.

Yes, that is what I found too, and these types of hashbang lines [are allowed](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/276751/is-space-allowed-between-and-bin-bash-in-shebang).  The question is should these be allowed in conda package or removed by `conda-build`.

---

@msarahan commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264683203)

I think all the code related to this is in Conda.  I'm transferring the issue over there.  Please ping me as necessary.

issue_title: Terminal throws error message everytime I cd to a git repository
issue_body:
@simmimourya1 commented on [Sat Nov 12 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1529)

I used anaconda2 for a project. I don't remember the first time when it threw this error because at that point it didn't affect the work flow and everything was working fine. I tried to fix it but couldn't find any work around. It still doesn't affect anything but this message  pops up every time I `cd` to a local git repository:
`Error: too few arguments, must supply command line package specs or --file`

![screenshot from 2016-11-13 07 03 32](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8150326/20243125/86e7fa7c-a96f-11e6-8928-64faee826845.png)

And it is bugging me. 

No error is thrown if I am not inside a git repository. 

![screenshot from 2016-11-13 07 03 52](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8150326/20243127/921228dc-a96f-11e6-9533-86b6d8f0e544.png)

Any clue?
 Thanks a lot for reading this!







---

@msarahan commented on [Sat Nov 12 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1529#issuecomment-260160988)

Sorry, I don't see how this is related to conda-build.  I'm moving this issue to conda.  I think it probably has something to do with your zsh customization, which is trying to run conda to figure out when environment you're in somehow.  I don't think this is anything standard, so unfortunately, you're probably going to need to figure this out yourself.

issue_title: Virtual env creation with python3 produces "TypeError: 'Process' object is not callable"
issue_body:
When creating a virtual environment using python3 as the `python` default I am getting a strange error message that seems to be non-fatal, but recommends that I refer it to this issues thread. Interested in (1) Where this comes from, (2) Whether it's affecting anything in my virtual env, and (3) how to fix it.

After running `conda update conda` to update my conda verion on OSX I run the following command to create a virtual environment with the most current python3 version as the `python` default: 

`conda create --name credijusto python=3`

This seems to work fine until the very end, when I run into the following error output: 

    An unexpected error has occurred, please consider sending the

issue_title: NoPackagesFoundError when accessing private package
issue_body:
We have a private package on Anaconda.org that CI normally downloads by adding a channel like so:

`conda config --add channels "https://conda.anaconda.org/t/%ANACONDA_TOKEN%/clinicalgraphics"`

This stopped working`for when upating from conda 4.1.12 to 4.2.6.

Here's `conda info -a` from when it was still working:
```
             platform : win-64
        conda version : 4.1.12
    conda-env version : 2.5.2
  conda-build version : 2.0.1
       python version : 3.5.2.final.0

Pandas out_flavor for better ctable performance
issue_body:
In this issue I want to make the case for the extension of the effect of `out_flavor` to `__getitem__()` (and related functions) and the introduction of a `pandas` outflavor. While I appreciate the rationale for limiting bcolz to the numpy data model I believe the possible performance improvements with pandas merit consideration.

I would be very interested to know if this has any chance for inclusion in bcolz, since the effort would be non-trivial for a clean implementation.

### Executive Summary ###
 - bcolz is a columnar (albeit chunked) data container
 - thus after decompression of each chunk, the data for each column is (chunk-)contiguous
 - currently however, this column-contiguous data is forced into a row-major memory structure (numpy structured array)
 - this has two consequences:
   1. the contiguous column-data has to be copied in row-sized chunks which is much slower than en-bloc copying of contiguous data,
   2. the data has to be copied (rather than being decompressed directly into the "proper" place)
 - Moving to a column-major memory layout (e.g. pandas DataFrame) would provide:
   1. much faster assignment of data to colums (which is a very common operation with `ctable`)
   2. opens the possibility for avoiding repeated copy operations in the first place
   3. ctable would be able to really leverage multi-core machines. Currently the numpy structured array is a bottleneck for `__getitem__()` and the like: #174
   4.  improve down-stream performance of data analysis as much data analysis is carried out along column rather than along rows
 -  due to the similar data access model of numpy structured arrays and pandas dataframes most code could probably remain unchanged after the output data object is initialised either as `numpy` or `pandas`
 
### Evidence of column assignment bottleneck ###
Note: for the sake of simplicity, when I use the term "column" in relation to numpy structured arrays, I refer to the fields of the (single column) structured array that bcolz uses to store its output column.

Numpy structured arrays (as used by bcolz) are inherently row-major. It is impossible to change this as far as I can see. This means that column assignment is fairly slow:
```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: M,N=int(1e7),10

# make a row-major numpy array

In [4]: A1=np.zeros((M,N),'f')

In [9]: dt=np.dtype(','.join(['f' for _ in range(N)]))

# make a structured numpy array

In [10]: A2=np.zeros((M,),dtype=dt)

In [11]: X=np.arange(M+0.0)

### simulate ctable __getitem()__ operation:
# assignment to a row-major numpy array column for column

In [13]: %timeit for n in range(N):A1[:,n]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 2.36 s per loop

# assignment of structured array field for field

In [15]: %timeit for n in dt.names: A2[n]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 2.36 s per loop
```

Significant speedup (factor 6.5!) can be achieved by moving to a column-major memory layout for the numpy array:
```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: M,N=int(1e7),10

# make a colum-major numpy array
In [3]: A1=np.zeros((M,N),'f', 'F')

In [4]: dt=np.dtype(','.join(['f' for _ in range(N)]))

# make a structured numpy array

In [5]: A2=np.zeros((M,),dtype=dt)

In [6]: X=np.arange(M+0.0)

### simulate ctable __getitem()__ operation:
# assignment to a column-major numpy array column for column

In [8]: %timeit for n in range(N):A1[:,n]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 374 ms per loop

# assignment of structured array field for field

In [9]: %timeit for n in dt.names: A2[n]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 2.43 s per loop
```

### Why Pandas DataFrames would help ###
While moving to column-major numpy arrays would be the ideal solution, this is obviously not an option: they require the entire array to have a homogeneous dtype.

Pandas DataFrame however supports columns of different dtypes and by design stores data in column-major order. (As I remember because Wes McKinney said that most of his data analysis happened along columns, though I cannot find the reference. That said I think the following article in which he explains his reasons for not choosing numpy structured arrays is interesting: [Wes McKinney: A Roadmap for Rich Scientific Data Structures in Python](http://wesmckinney.com/blog/a-roadmap-for-rich-scientific-data-structures-in-python/))

In addition, the choice of column-major ordering permits size-mutability: columns can be added without copying the data. This fits well with the ctable column-store philosopy. With numpy structured arrays, the entire array has to be copied (almost entry by entry as far as I can see) to add a new column.

Due to these advantages, column-major ordering is used my many well-known dedicated number crunching environments, among others: Fortran, MATLAB and R

Note: Making a pandas DataFrame for an already existing numpy structured array returned by ctable is not an option either: again, effectively element by element copying of the data is required.

### Evidence of performance improvements with Pandas ###

Instantiation of Pandas DataFrames is admittedly an issue with smaller databases. Leaving this issue aside for the moment, one can see that assignment to an (instantiated DataFrame) is much faster:

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import pandas as pd

In [3]: M,N=int(1e7),10

In [4]: A1=np.zeros((M,N),'f', 'F')

In [5]: dt=np.dtype(','.join(['f' for _ in range(N)]))

In [6]: A2=np.zeros((M,),dtype=dt)

In [7]: X=np.arange(M+0.0)

# make a pandas DataFrame

In [8]: df_A1 = pd.DataFrame(index=xrange(M), columns=range(N), dtype='f')

### simulate ctable __getitem()__ operation:
# assignment to a column-major numpy array column for column

In [9]: %timeit for n in range(N): A1[:,n]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 369 ms per loop

# assignment to a structured numpy array field for field

In [10]: %timeit for n in dt.names: A2[n]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 2.36 s per loop

# (optimized) assignment to a pandas DataFrame column for column

In [8]: %timeit for n in range(N): df_A1._data.blocks[0].values[n,:]=X
1 loops, best of 3: 364 ms per loop
```

Instantiation of the DataFrame will probably be an issue. My gut reaction is that it should be possible to instantiate (and cache) an empty DataFrame with the correct structure and then for each `__getitem__()` call shallow-copy this template and assign new data arrays. This should help with the instantiation overhead, since the column makeup of a ctable instance usually does not change too often during a program.

### Down the road ###
In the long run it might be worth getting rid of the memory copies altogether for DataFrame out_flavor and decopressing the chunks directly into the arrays backing the DataFrame. This would likely lead to further performance improvements (though smaller as the remaining memory copies would already be efficient en-bloc copies).

issue_title: Pileup is wrong with spliced reads
issue_body:
If you have  read spliced across a region, pileup will still happily report reference and query positions
even for positions where the read actually has no aligned base.

Toy example: Should not output anything, outputs [(100, 2), (101, 2)...]


import pysam

sam = pysam.AlignmentFile("shu.bam", 'wb', reference_names = ['chr1'], reference_lengths=[100000])
r = pysam.AlignedSegment()
r.query_name = "test1"
r.seq = "ACCT"
r.qual = "xxxx"
r.pos = 100
r.cigartuples = [ (0,2), (3, 500), (0,2)]
sam.write(r)
sam.close()
pysam.index('shu.bam')

sam = pysam.AlignmentFile('shu.bam')
for p in sam.pileup('chr1', 100, 200, truncate=True, stepper='nofilter'):
    for read in p.pileups:
        print 'reference pos', p.reference_pos, 'query pos', read.query_position

issue_title: EXTERNAL TERMINAL NOT WORKING
[Bug] Context menu entries does not work at mouse but at cursor position
issue_body:
Using of a context menu entry should always use the mouse position, not the cursor position.

The easiest implementation may be:

- disable screen updates (if possible)
- save current cursor position
- set cursor position based on mouse cursor
- do the requested action
- restore cursor position
- enable screen updates (if possible)

This applies to nearly all context menu options: (un)intend, calculate offset, duplicate line, ...

These should be done at cursor position / selection when used from edit menu but at mouse position (either cursor or selection, whatever is below the cursor) when used from context menu.

## System information


- OpenCobolIDE: 4.6.5
- Python: 3.4.3 (32bits)
- Qt: 5.5.0
- PyQt: 5.5
- pyqode.core: 2.6.8
- pyqode.cobol: 2.6.3

issue_title: [Enhancement/Bug] Add option for copybook search paths
issue_body:
It's possible to add copybook search paths by specifying -I <directory> in the compiler settings in the free line widget. For example:

-I cpy -I ../cpy -I /path/to/copybooks

This works for the compiler but as OCIDE doesn't see them all these copybooks are underlined with error message "No such file or directory".

I suggest to add an option in the compiler settings listing only the directories for additional copybook search paths (separated by ; may be a good idea if the directories contain spaces)

It may could be a good idea to add an option for folding copybook names "lnone[default]/lower/upper" (and passing this to the compiler as -ffold-copy-lower/upper) for enabling OCIDE to find the files.

issue_title: Curator is very slow on cluster with many indices
issue_body:
We have a cluster with about 7000 indices, with new indices being created every day. We would like to run curator daily to assign indices to different nodes (SSD vs spinning disk), and adjust their replica settings, depending on the age of the index. However, when operating on a large number of indices (e.g. --older-than 90 days yields thousands of indices on our cluster), we find that curator spends a long time querying each individual index, checking it's status. Using --debug, I noticed that curator queries e.g. /_cat/indices/my-index-20151202?h=status&format=json for each index, which can 5 or even 10 seconds on our cluster. This means that a single execution can take 10 or more hours to complete, and we would like to run it more often than that.

Digging into the code, I've found this:

 ```python
    # This workaround using the _cat API is for AWS Elasticsearch, since it does
    # not allow users to poll the cluster state.  It may also be faster than
    # using the cluster state.
    if get_version(client) >= (1, 5, 0):
        indices = client.cat.indices(index=index_name, format='json', h='status')
        # This workaround is also for AWS, as the _cat API still returns text/plain
        # even with ``format='json'``.
        if not isinstance(indices, list):  # content-type: text/plain
            indices = json.loads(indices)
        (index_info,) = indices
        return index_info['status'] == 'close'
    else:
        index_metadata = client.cluster.state(
            index=index_name,
            metric='metadata',
        )
        return index_metadata['metadata']['indices'][index_name]['state'] == 'close'
```

I suppose this decision to query each index rather than using the cluster state is causing this slowness for us.

Is there a compromise that can be made, such as adding a --use-cluster-state (I'm sure a better name can be imagined) option?

Thanks.

issue_title: DNS cache in `aiohttp.TCPConnector` causes client requests to timeout when host address changes
issue_body:
Hi!

Using domain-based elasticsearch hosts that change their IP over time can cause `AsyncElasticsearch` client to timeout requests as the default DNS cache resolves the hostname with old address.
 
For example, with Elasticsearch Heroku Add-ons that run on AWS, the host URL doesn't change and looks like `https://abcdef-123455.eu-west-1.bonsaisearch.net`, but the IP address can change over time. Because of the DNS cache - which isn't flushed - the client tries to send requests to wrong host IP and failes.

Solution might be to pass explicit `use_dns_cache` param to `aiohttp.TCPConnector` [in the constructor of `AIOHttpConnection`](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py-async/blob/c76817dd24e4cfa0ced0fa13fa4a70721d649df0/elasticsearch_async/connection.py#L29). 
Additionally, because of the default DNS resolver being [thread-based](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/blob/9fbb7d708375e0ba01d435c1e8cf41912381c0fc/aiohttp/resolver.py#L17), a `dns_resolver` param could be used to specify own, for example `aiodns`-based [AsyncResolver](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/blob/9fbb7d708375e0ba01d435c1e8cf41912381c0fc/aiohttp/resolver.py#L47), although it was removed as default one because of compatibility [issues with IPv6 systems](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/issues/559).

I can submit a PR with this solution soon.
What do you think?

issue_title: No Longer a method to extract Activity Log for a resource
issue_body:
The `azure-monitor` python package contained the `MonitorClient`, capable of accessing `activity_logs` and filtering by resource_id, resource_group, etc. With `azure-monitor`'s deprecation, surely `azure-mgmt-monitor` would have this type of functionality? Nope. 

Even better, the hosted docs on microsoft's website state that the `MonitorManagementClient` of the `azure-mgmt-monitor` package _should_ be able to fetch `activity_logs`: [link](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/python/api/azure.mgmt.monitor.monitormanagementclient?view=azure-python). What gives microsoft? Why support so many language specific SDKs and keep them essentially out of sync with one another _and_ the PS cmdlets?

Just for fun, here is the output of `dir` on a MonitorManagementClient instantiated with the `azure.mgmt` modules on Python 2.7:

`[...<python_specific_outputs>..., 'activity_log_alerts', 'alert_rule_incidents', 'alert_rules', 'autoscale_settings','config','log_profiles','service_diagnostic_settings']`

Honestly, would love a Linus Torvalds-like audit of this repo, this is fourth inconsistency I've run into in a week's time between docs, code samples, the actual code, and the nuances of interfacing with sovereign clouds vs commercial.

issue_title: pip uninstall breaks the SDK
issue_body:
To reproduce:
```console
$ pip install azure==2.0.0rc6
Successfully installed azure-2.0.0rc6 
$ python -c "import azure.mgmt.compute; print('OK')"
OK
$ pip uninstall azure-mgmt-network
Successfully uninstalled azure-mgmt-network-0.30.0rc6
$ python -c "import azure.mgmt.compute"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named azure.mgmt.compute
```

This is because we have a packaging issue, and that _every_ package have their own copy of `azure/__init__.py`. So when uninstalling, this file is removed and it breaks the package.

Note that this can be seen only in Python 2.7, thanks to PEP420 on Python 3 (this is not on purpose...)

issue_title: Invalid predictions (transmat_ not respected)
issue_body:
As of f66a053e, there is something wrong with model prediction. As far as I can tell, the model `transmat_` is ignored.

The test script below produces impossible predictions, where the state exits the final state (i.e. 0 transition probability to any other state). The first line is the sample, the second is the correct state sequence, and the final line is the predicted state sequence. State 2 has transition probabilities of `0 0 1`, so the state sequence `2 1` should be impossible, but it is still predicted.

```
[ 0.97463307  0.98580524  0.96502726  0.94767963  0.96783919  0.02015515
  0.97280737  1.05605478  1.01784615 -0.01791463  0.02308386  0.0117951
  0.01688058 -0.00290845  0.03611139 -0.03572094 -0.02688102  0.0303838
  0.02761991 -0.00352225]
[0 0 0 0 0 1 0 0 0 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2]
[0 0 0 0 0 2 0 0 0 1 2 1 2 1 2 1 2 1 2 1]
```

Test script:
```
from hmmlearn import hmm
import numpy as np
from itertools import accumulate

def predictMultiple(model, *traces):
    lengths = list(map(len, traces))
    data = np.concatenate(traces)
    states = model.predict(data, lengths)

    splits = list(accumulate(lengths))
    return np.split(states, splits)[:-1]

np.random.seed(1)

model = hmm.GaussianHMM(
    n_components=3, params='st', init_params='',
)

model.transmat_ = np.array(
    [[0.8, 0.1, 0.1],
     [0.1, 0.9, 0.0],
     [0.0, 0.0, 1.0]]
)
model.means_ = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0]).reshape(-1, 1)
model.covars_ = np.array([0.001, 0.001, 0.001]).reshape(-1, 1)
model.startprob_ = np.array([0.8, 0.1, 0.1])

samples, states = model.sample(20)
prediction = model.predict(samples)

print(samples[:, 0])
print(states)
print(prediction)

np.testing.assert_equal(states, prediction)
```

issue_title: extra_compile_args=["-O3"]
issue_body:
On Windows (conda/gcc), the default compilation switch is "-O", which yields a much slower extension module than what can be obtained after compiling with "-O3".  I would thus suggest adding `extra_compile_args=["-O3"]` to the `setup.py` so that this is used by default.
Not sure whether this would affect those using the MSVC compiler though.

issue_title: Stream messages ignored or displayed under incorrect code cells
issue_body:
After executing a cell that produces `stream` messages, Jupyter will stop displaying the `stream` messages associated with the cell's `parent_header` `msg_id` after executing another cell.

This is leading to an [issue](https://github.com/ibm-et/spark-kernel/issues/129) in the Spark Kernel where stream messages from a previous cell are arriving, but not being displayed. In the following example, notice that messages with `parent_header` `msg_id` A81... continue to arrive but are not displayed:
![spark_kernel_example](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/680489/10206557/2ba94bea-678d-11e5-922c-266a3bf92fd3.gif)

It looks like the Python Kernel uses the `parent_header` `msg_id` from the most recently executed cell when sending stream messages. As a result, stream messages may 'switch' to another cell after execution:
![python_kernel_example](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/680489/10206487/b7ce567a-678c-11e5-8314-34df366bb9ac.gif)

Is this the desired behavior?

issue_title: Lint to check post messages are translatable is throwing false positives
issue_body:
The lint `translation-required` is intended to detect not-translatable messages withing methods like `message_post`, like in this code:
```
self.message_post(body='some message'))
```
And suggest using the function `_()` to make them translatable.

However, codes like the following are triggering false positives:
```
self.message_post(
    body=_('Error during the process: %s') % message,
    subtype='account.mt_invoice_validated')
```

Showing the following error message:
```
l10n_mx_edi_payslip/models/hr_payslip.py:264: [C8107(translation-required), HrPayslip.l10n_mx_edi_log_error] String parameter on "message_post" requires translation. Use body=_((_('Error during the process: %s')) % (message))
```

Not sure if this has anything to do with `message_post` and `_(...)` being located on different lines.

issue_title: TypeError: bad operand type for abs(): 'FloatNode'
issue_body:
Hi, your work is totally amazing :) I have been using it for a few weeks already and I faced this "bad operand type" issue recently. I have double-checked everything and I'm not using numpy anywhere. I'm little confused with what exactly might have went wrong and I'd be happy to present any working (well, not working, actually :) ) example, but right now it's just unclear what exactly to copy-paste;

Here's the actual loss, `bk == autograd.numpy` here, `data` and `theta` are `namedtuple` (the packing-unpacking part works just fine with `autograd` here, tested with other losses):

    def predict(self, theta, data):
        const = assert_arr_shape({
            data.X.shape: ('batch_size', self.n_steps, self.n_input_dim),
            data.y.shape: ('batch_size', self.n_hidden_dim)
        })
        h_prev = bk.repeat(theta.h_0[:, bk.newaxis], const.batch_size, axis=1).T
        activation = lambda x: bk.maximum(0, 1-x)
        to_stack = []
        for i in range(self.n_steps):
            input2hidden = bk.dot(data.X[:, i, :], theta.W_input_to_hidden)
            hidden2hidden = bk.dot(h_prev, theta.W_hidden_to_hidden)
            before_activation = (input2hidden + hidden2hidden)
            h_prev = activation(before_activation)
            to_stack.append(h_prev)
        output = bk.stack(to_stack, axis=1)
        assert_arr_shape({output.shape: (None, self.n_steps, self.n_hidden_dim)})
        
        return output

    def loss(self, theta, data):
        pred = self.predict(theta, data)
        last_step_pred = pred[:, -1, :]
        loss = bk.sum(bk.abs(last_step_pred - data.y))
        return loss

The forward pass (`loss(x)`, `x`-ravel'ed everything) executes just fine, but when I try computing the gradient (`autograd.grad(loss)(x)`), this exception raises.

It does indeed tries to apply `abs()` on the np.ndarray that has `FloatNode` objects instead of actual floats in it.

issue_title: bug: requests.request running endless in some cases
issue_body:
My router changes his ip every three days, and then this sdk does not reconnect to the server.
[This](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-python-sdk/blob/master/matrix_client/api.py#L558) request method does not return in that case, and so there is also no exception which could be handled.

Solution: add a timeout to the request.
I can do a pull request, but there are some points to discuss:
- The timeout has to be larger than the timeout defined in the query_params. (if available). Should we use 60 seconds by default (if no timeout is available), otherwise 2*query_params['timeout'] (`request_timeout = 2 * query_params.get('timeout', 60.0)`)
- Should we handle the possible timeout exception in listen_forever by default?

Moreover I have to search whether there are more requests in this code, and whether it is useful to add a timeout.

issue_title: Tweak the helper methods
issue_body:
It's really easy to call a helper and pass in the wrong kind of arguments. For
example, it's really tempting to write this:

```python
content_view.publish({'content_view_id': 5})
```

But that's totally wrong. That's the same as:

```python
content_view.publish(synchronous={'content_view_id': 5})
```

Let's make the `synchronous` argument a keyword argument. This is a breaking
change, which sucks. But thankfully, it's a fairly straightforward breaking
change to deal with, and it may help prevent many mis-uses of the helper
methods. We're changing method signatures from this:

```python
my_method(synchronous=True, **kwargs)
```

To this:

```python
my_method(**kwargs)
```

At this point, all of the helper methods would have exactly two custom kwargs:
`synchronous` and `timeout`. (Assuming #218 is implemented.)

A second possible change is to make each of the helper methods have a signature
that matches the underlying method being called. As an example, the `publish`
method calls `post`. If both the first and second suggestions are accepted, it
changes like so:

```python
publish(synchronous=True, **kwargs)
publish(**kwargs)
publish(data=None, json=None, **kwargs)
```

Thankfully, this second proposed change is not breaking. Given that the
Satellite/Foreman API deals exclusively with JSON, I'm not sure how much of an
improvement this second change would yield for clients. But it's something to
consider.

issue_title: subnet parsing behavior changed in 0.78.2
issue_body:
Before, I used to be able to use shorthand such as:

```
sshuttle -r ... 192.168.208/20
```

Or:

```
sshuttle -r ... 10/8
```

And these would be expanded to `192.168.208.0/20` or `10.0.0.0/8` respectively.

Since https://github.com/sshuttle/sshuttle/commit/c4a41ada09ec6fbfeb1783eb0269cad013842982 it appears that this behavior has changed when piping the address through `socket.getaddrinfo`:

```
>>> socket.getaddrinfo('10', 0, 0, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
[(2, 1, 6, '', ('0.0.0.10', 0))]
```

`socket.getaddrinfo` seems to not zero fill on the right hand side like the old code used to do.

I'm not sure if this is intended/correct behavior, or if it just worked magically before by accident, but it seems like it'd be relatively easy to restore this behavior by padding the string with `.0`'s before passing into `socket.getaddrinfo`. I'll submit a PR to go along with this.

issue_title: Parallel-test locking, OR multiprocessing-based backend or fork?
issue_body:
TL;DR: Any way to run tests in parallel but let them coordinate with locks/semaphores?

(If this should have gone to a mailing list or something, please do let me know.)

I'd _really_ like to use pytest for some live-system integration tests, but I'd also really like to run tests in parallel -- with a little bit of locking for one unfortunately-half-serial part of the application.

For example (pseudocode):
```
user_locks = collections.defaultdict(multiprocessing.Lock)
def test_1():
    # do setup stuff
    with user_locks["test_user"]:
        # Write to test_user's work-directory and kick off a slow process that reads those files.

def test_2():
    # do setup stuff
    with user_locks["test_user"]:
        # This CANNOT happen at the same time as test_1's with-lock block.

def test_3():
    # do setup stuff
    with user_locks["other_test_user"]:
        # This CAN happen concurrently with test_1 or test_2
```

So far, the closest I've come to this with another framework was using the `mp` module in nose2, which uses `multiprocessing`.  That seems to work appropriately with `multiprocessing.Lock`, but it doesn't have that special pytest flavor.

So, here are my options so far:
1. Maybe there's a way to do this with `pytest-xdist` that I didn't notice?
2. Perhaps it's feasible to add a `multiprocessing` back-end to `pytest-xdist`?
3. Perhaps it wouldn't be too hard to make my own `multiprocessing`-based `pytest` plugin.
4. I could always bite the bullet and use `nose2`.

Which option seems more reasonable to you?  If 2, please consider this as a request for it.

issue_title: .net core 1.1 IFormFileCollection files Performance issue
Use of created nested types in dynamic assembly builders cause TypeLoadExceptions
issue_body:
When attempting to generate a dynamic type that contains a nested type, use of the type to generate fields on the parent type fails with a [TypeLoadException][exception] if the type has been realized using `GetTypeInfo()`.

This issue is not present on Mono, and the type can be successfully created. Furthermore, defining the type as a non-nested type in the dynamic module also succeeds.

A minimal [reproduction project][repro-project] has been included which targets both the full framework and .NET Core 2.0. The aforementioned exception is thrown at line 23 when the project is ran using .NET Core 2.0.

To run the project, unzip the file and open it in your IDE of choice, then compile and run. If you are not using an IDE, execute the following commands:

```
unzip Scratchpad.zip
cd Scratchpad
dotnet run --project Scratchpad --framework netcoreapp2.0 // for .net core
dotnet run --project Scratchpad --framework net461 // for .net 4.6.1
```

[repro-project]: https://github.com/dotnet/core/files/1947057/Scratchpad.zip
[exception]: https://gist.github.com/Nihlus/63eb35f3ee3a2f38456002c60bb5d057

issue_title: adding -delete option to remove the cert files
issue_body:
Hello,

Do you think you can add an -delete option to let's encrypt?

My context is this: 
From a hosting control panel, users will be able to create an owncloud instance.
When deleting their owncloud instance, I should delete also the certificates files in /etc/letsencrypt folder (I'm not talking about revocation as I can let the certs expire).

Deleting them seems important as by not deleting myself the files in /etc/letsencrypt/{archives,live,renewal}, if I understand correctly, the renewal of the certs will renew all the certs, included the certs for (sub)domains that don't exist anymore.

I must say I'm not sure how the renewal system works, if it tests the domain validity or the host file presence before renewing it. But still, you can have a host file for a domain name that doesn't exist anymore (not very good, but it can happen) and you can have a catchall for subdomains that show a generic page or use the (sub)domain for another purpose.

The only post I found on that topic (https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/correct-way-to-completely-remove-issued-certificate-s-for-a-domain/7409/4) doesn't have an answer on this problem.

If I manage the deletion by myself, deleting all the files /etc/letsencrypt/ subdirs, what about if something changes in the way let's encrypt manage the files (names / placements / ..).
The script will be broken.

Maybe there is another way:
If, by using the revoke option, the renewal feature won't renew the revoked certs, I can live with that.
But in that case, if later, a user want to re-use the same (sub)domain, is it possible to reactivate the certs? (didn't see anything about that in the doc. The renew feature doesn't say anything about this case)

So, is it possible to add such a feature?

And by the way, let's encrypt is a wonderful project. Thanks to you all.

issue_title: the help message should be more explicit about the effect of the -d option
issue_body:
#### Hypothetical language

A suggestion, possibly mostly correct:

If the -d option is mentioned more than once on the command line, a *single* certificate will be created to cover all the domains that were mentioned.  In particular:
 * The first-mentioned domain will be the Subject CN of the certificate.
 * All the domains (including the first) will be version-3 Subject Alternative Names on the certificate.
 * The certificate will be stored with a filename derived from the first-mentioned domain.

#### Question

Suppose there are multiple inequivalent certificates having the same first domain but different additional domains.  There is the potential for conflict with the filename.  How is this conflict resolved?

#### Suggestion

The stated goal is to make the process easy.  To this end, it would be helpful for somebody who understands the process to document it in the help message.

issue_title: [Ask About Server License]
issue_body:
Hello, Gene

I have question regarding server license. If we run Tinode on our own servers to serve our public users, does this count as act of _"distribution"_?

So we would like to integrate Tinode with our own existing system. To do so we need to modify Tinode in the way that it would expose our internal architecture.

As far as I know in GPL we need also to make our modified source code public whenever the distribution of the software is made. Yet in our case it is impossible to do so after the modification is done. 

So we would like to confirm first what is the meaning of _"distribution"_ here for Tinode.

Thanks a lot, Gene

issue_title: Dual-mode authenticate appears broken
issue_body:
We have a deployment of pypiserver where we want to password protect updates, but want to leave download and list operations public. The docs (e.g. ``$ pypi-server --help``) say:

> For example to password-protect package downloads (in addition to uploads)
> while leaving listings public, give:
> -P foo/htpasswd.txt  -a update,download

I conclude from that statement that we should use "-P foo/htpasswd.txt  -a update". Is this correct? With this config, I would expect to be able to browse to https://localhost:[port]/simple with no authentication but need auth to register/submit packages. However, on the current master, using these settings, trying to browse to this URL, I get a HTTP 401.

I put together a quick test case (below) based on your great test suite to check these assumptions and ran a git bisect between v1.2.0 and master.

```bash
$ git bisect start HEAD v1.2.0 --
$ git bisect run ./script.sh
3d37c009997ec8f199e54524641726fea260906e is the first bad commit
commit 3d37c009997ec8f199e54524641726fea260906e
Author: Kyle Hornberg <kyle.hornberg@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 29 07:35:46 2016 -0500

    Add matrix auth

:040000 040000 bdddda1c65ca6fad7fafd944467db5d402931477 424a7dd7630432581a3c8f454036598c741f5074 M	pypiserver
:040000 040000 a962432e4cae100dceba73d35bcae12ed79f8f9e 7c047a288d92226aa2fd13be80ad0b9cd722d5b9 M	tests
bisect run success
```

Looks like the addition of the matrix auth broke some functionality the the classic list-based auth config? Is there a way to accomplish the config behavior that we want using the matrix auth style?

Cheers,

~James

script.sh:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

py.test -k stuff
```

tests/test_stuff.py
```python
import contextlib
import subprocess
import time
import sys
from urllib.request import urlopen
from textwrap import dedent

import pytest

from .test_server import _build_url, empty_packdir, port, _BUFF_SIZE, _port, \
    SLEEP_AFTER_SRV, Srv, _kill_server, project, package, pypirc_file, chdir, \
    _run_python


def _run_server(packdir, port, authed, other_cli=''):
    pswd_opt_choices = {
        True: "-Ptests/htpasswd.a.a -a update,download,list",
        False: "-P. -a.",
        'partial': "-Ptests/htpasswd.a.a -a update"
    }
    pswd_opts = pswd_opt_choices[authed]
    cmd = "%s -m pypiserver.__main__ -vvv --overwrite -p %s %s %s %s" % (
        sys.executable, port, pswd_opts, other_cli, packdir)
    proc = subprocess.Popen(cmd.split(), bufsize=_BUFF_SIZE)
    time.sleep(SLEEP_AFTER_SRV)
    assert proc.poll() is None

    return Srv(proc, int(port), packdir)


@contextlib.contextmanager
def new_server(packdir, port, authed=False, other_cli=''):
    srv = _run_server(packdir, port,
                      authed=authed, other_cli=other_cli)
    try:
        yield srv
    finally:
        _kill_server(srv)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("pkg_frmt", ['bdist', 'bdist_wheel'])
def test_setuptoolsUpload_partial_authed(empty_packdir, port, project, package,
                                 pkg_frmt, monkeypatch):
    url = _build_url(port)
    with pypirc_file(dedent("""\
            [distutils]
            index-servers: test

            [test]
            repository: %s
            username: a
            password: a
        """ % url)):
        with new_server(empty_packdir, port, authed='partial'):
            with chdir(project.strpath):
                cmd = "setup.py -vvv %s register -r test upload -r test" % pkg_frmt
                for i in range(5):
                    print('++Attempt #%s' % i)
                    assert _run_python(cmd) == 0
            time.sleep(SLEEP_AFTER_SRV)
    assert len(empty_packdir.listdir()) == 1


# Ensure that we can hit the package listing without any authentication
def test_authed_upload_open_download(empty_packdir, port):
    url = _build_url(port) + '/simple'
    with new_server(empty_packdir, port, authed='partial'):
        resp = urlopen(url)
        assert resp.getcode() == 200
```

issue_title: Memoize time zones
issue_body:
A huge amount of the time zone functions are called multiple times with identical inputs and expecting identical outputs, and the whole concept of a `tzinfo` is that it should be more-or-less a singleton, so there's a huge potential for memoization that is not realized.

Ideally, we'll stub out an equivalent of `functools.lrucache` so that it's backward compatible. I will update this list later, but here's a starter of things that need to be memoized:

[] `gettz`
[] `tzfile` (`utcoffset`, `dst`, `tzname`)
[] `tzical` (already done to some extent, but we should convert this over to the same way everything else is)

Likely all non-static offsets will need `utcoffset`, `dst` and `tzname` memoized.

The big concern with doing this is that it will likely increase memory consumption in downstream applications, some of which may be operating in memory-constrained environments. I'm thinking the best way to address this is to query an environment variable at import-time that sets the length of the caches to something other than default.

issue_title: Add weeks argument to relativedelta
issue_body:
Migrating issue from launchpad [issue #727525](https://bugs.launchpad.net/dateutil/+bug/727525).

You can read the full exchange there, but the gist is that while there's a keyword for "weeks" in relativedelta, you cannot retrieve the number of weeks in a relativedelta object as you can for the other time periods.

The solution would be to offer a `weeks` property that returns `self.days // 7`. Seems reasonable.

issue_title: rrule.__str__ doesn't render wkst properly
issue_body:
`rrule.__str__` is rendering `WKST` as an integer instead of as a weekday. For example:

```python
>>> rule = dateutil.rrule.rrulestr("DTSTART:20000101\nFREQ=WEEKLY;WKST=SU")
>>> str(rule)
'DTSTART:20000101T000000\nFREQ=WEEKLY;WKST=6'
```
This breaks compatibility with RFC5545 and also with `rrulestr`:
```python
>>> dateutil.rrule.rrulestr(str(rule))
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
ValueError: invalid 'WKST': 6
```

issue_title: RPC errors objects are unpicklable.
issue_body:
This code
```
import pickle
from telethon.errors.rpc_error_list import FloodWaitError  # For example

error = FloodWaitError()

a = pickle.dumps(error)
b = pickle.loads(a)
```
will raise:
```
    ...
    b = pickle.loads(a)
TypeError: __init__() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given
```
It is due to Python's default implementation of `__reduce__` method of `BaseException` class.
**Solution:**
Provide custom implementation of `__reduce__` method to the base `RPCError` class:
```
# in telethon/errors/rpc_base_errors.py
class RPCError(Exception):
    """Base class for all Remote Procedure Call errors."""
    code = None
    message = None
        
    def __reduce__(self):
        return type(self), ()
```
Then it works fine.

issue_title: Add support for videos as featured images
Allow articles to have a featured video
issue_body:
We want to extend the Article model to support a `featured_video` field in addition to the existing `featured_image` field.

Backend requirements:
- Add a `featured_video` field to the [Article model](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/modules/content/models.py#L246)
  - Should be a foreign key to a new `VideoAttachment` model, similar to [ImageAttachment](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/modules/content/models.py#L480-L490)
- Extend the [ArticleSerializer](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/api/serializers.py#L388) to support the new `featured_video` field

Frontend requirements:
- Add an interface to the article editor that allows the user to set a featured video (in addition to a featured image)
  - This can be implemented as a new input called `VideoSelectInput`
  - Unlike the [ImageInput](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/inputs/ImageInput.js), it doesn't need to open the image manager modal and can just be a simple `ItemSelectInput` (similar to [ArticleSelectInput](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/inputs/selects/ArticleSelectInput.js))
  - After `VideoSelectInput` is created, it can be added to the [FeaturedImageTab](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/Editor/tabs/FeaturedImageTab.js) component of the article editor

issue_title: Not able to connect after last kernel upgrade
issue_body:
Hi
I upgraded ubuntu today, which included a new kernel, and since then, I haven't been able to connect to a 3D printer using pyserial. I receive
```
SerialException: device reports readiness to read but returned no data (device disconnected?)
```
Just in case, the fact that this is a 3D printer is transparent. It just publishes a virtual serial device the printer connects to.
My environment is:
```
Python 2.7.6
Kernel 3.13.0-65-generic #105-Ubuntu SMP Mon Sep 21 18:51:54 UTC 2015 i686 i686 i686 GNU/Linux
$ dpkg -l | fgrep pyserial
ii  python-serial                                                             2.6-1build1                                         all          pyserial - module encapsulating access for the serial port
```
The device is connected using the USB to serial driver. Tried both using a tipical USB and an additional FTDI programmer to ensure it was not because of the cable or the board input.
I'm not sure if this is a pyserial bug, but decided it would be better if you evaluated it. I could connect using screen command executing:
screen /dev/ttyACM0 115200
or using the external FTDI programmer
screen /dev/ttyUSB0 115200
In both cases I could connect. so, it doesn't seem to be a global scale problem. However, I couldn't connect using pyserial applications, like printrun/pronterface (a python based 3D printer client) or miniterm.py.
As a workaround, I modified the file serialposix.py, and commented a few lines:
```python
            if not ready:
                break   # timeout
            buf = os.read(self.fd, size-len(read))
            # read should always return some data as select reported it was
            # ready to read when we get to this point.
            if not buf:
                # Disconnected devices, at least on Linux, show the
                # behavior that they are always ready to read immediately
                # but reading returns nothing.
                raise SerialException('device reports readiness to read but returned no data (device disconnected?)')
            read.extend(buf)
        return bytes(read)
```
Changed those to:
```python
            if not ready:
                break   # timeout
            buf = os.read(self.fd, size-len(read))
            # read should always return some data as select reported it was
            # ready to read when we get to this point.
            #if not buf:
                # Disconnected devices, at least on Linux, show the
                # behavior that they are always ready to read immediately
                # but reading returns nothing.
                #raise SerialException('device reports readiness to read but returned no data (device disconnected?)')
            read.extend(buf)
        return bytes(read)
```
and then it worked.
I know those lines should be there, but they seem to be related to the symptoms of not being able to connect to the device. Maybe this helps you to find out what the problem is, and if this has to do with pyserial, the kernel or another project.
Thanks in advance!

Dear All DRMAA Python Users and Developers,

I have the abovementioned issue, and have not been able to direct the standard error messages out to the desired file path whenever I run a process on my distributed SGE-controlled cluster. In my Python code, I have the following statements that are:

    jt.outputPath = ':' + self.stdout_log_filepath

And in my debugging I have inserted print statements before and after the `errorPath` line to ensure that it was actually executed. Somehow it seems like DRMAA has not separated the standard error from the standard output streams, and the standard error stream gets written out to the same output file path as the standard error, and thus both std error and std output both go into `self.stdout_log_filepath`.
