issue_title: forced filters vs default filters
issue_body:
Some filters are necessary to be present, in order to reduce the amount of data a grid is processing. Otherwise the results could take a very long time to complete. In other cases, they are simply user helpers which should be allowed to be cleared.

What we need is the concept of a "forced" filter, introduced by having is_forced=True in the filter constructor. Forced filters will need to require a default op, and in the UI would remove the possibility of disabling them.

"Default" filters would then need the grid to tell them if they should be processed with the default op. If the user has selected other filters, the default should be allowed to lapse.

I had a kludge in place from when I reported the `fs.sshfs` issue, and forgot that I had extended it to `fs.ftpfs`. I just refactored my openers (now I have a better unsderstanding of the PyFilesystem2 API) and I dropped that kludge :-/

issue_title: Contributor Guidelines for updating snapshots
issue_body:
So some of our tests involve jest snapshots which have a good amount of [best practices](https://jestjs.io/docs/en/snapshot-testing#best-practices) around how to work with them:[jest-docs](https://jestjs.io/docs/en/snapshot-testing).

We also have some conventions and best practices specific to nteract. 

For example, we are concerned with the declarative JSX after it has been processed by utilities like styled-jsx, not an actual DOM render. 

Also we should document how to update the snapshots:

```
yarn test --updateSnapshot
``` 

I'll be happy to make a PR once I know where such a PR should actually live… I'm thinking it might be in [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/nteract/nteract/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md) but it's already pretty complicated. 

@willingc I'm pretty sure you were working on some of the developer docs… is there somewhere else this info should go as well?

issue_title: ELFTLSObject lies about its size
issue_body:
When ELFTLSObject has to be rebased in Loader._map_object, it computes size of object with 
```python
obj_size = obj.max_addr - obj.min_addr
```
then the clemory is [mapped](https://github.com/angr/cle/blob/master/cle/loader.py#L708) but the clemory max_addr is bigger that obj.max_addr, so if you try to rebase another object right after this one this would fail.

That fix seem to work for me but I haven't tested it much
```diff
diff --git a/cle/backends/tls/elf_tls.py b/cle/backends/tls/elf_tls.py
index 680d965..c973a75 100644
--- a/cle/backends/tls/elf_tls.py
+++ b/cle/backends/tls/elf_tls.py
@@ -119,7 +119,7 @@ class ELFTLSObject(TLSObject):
 
     @property
     def max_addr(self):
-        return self.mapped_base + TLS_TOTAL_HEAD_SIZE + self.max_data + 2*self.arch.bytes + 2*self.arch.bytes*self.max_modules
+        return self.mapped_base+self.memory.max_addr
 
     def get_addr(self, module_id, offset):
         """
```

issue_title: Explorer.run() vs Explorer.run(1) vs PathGroup.explore()
issue_body:
First of all, let me say that angr is an amazing tool! Thank you for releasing it. I have already started to play with it (e.g., see my pull request in angr-doc https://github.com/angr/angr-doc/pull/75). I have some doubts about Explorer and PathGroup.

The documentation states that:

> Pathgroups are supposed to replace surveyors.Explorer, being more clever and efficient. 

I am observing that this may be not true in some cases. Also, I think that the behavior of Explorer is inconsistent wrt PathGroup, but also wrt to itself. Let me explain why.

1) Explorer can be used like this (A):
```
ex = proj.surveyors.Explorer(..., found=XXX, avoid = YYY, ...)
ex.run() //
```
Or (B): 
```
ex = proj.surveyors.Explorer(..., found=XXX, avoid = YYY, ...)
while True:
   ex.run(1)
   if len(ex.found) >= 1:
        break
```
I was expecting that the result will be same with (A) or (B) but it's not. Explorer is selecting active paths (among active+spilled paths) based on how many times the next instruction of a path have been executed (see Explorer.path_comparator()). However, the instruction counter used by path_comparator() is updated only when Explorer.run() is called. This means that if you call many times run() like in (B), you may get a very different instruction counter that in (A) where you call run() only once. In my case, on one binary, (B) was able to hit a found path after ~4mins, while (A) did not hit a found path after ~20mins (I stopped it... I don't know if it is able to hit it sooner or later). I don't know when (A) is better of (B), or vice-versa. This was just my experience. I find this behavior a bit confusing or, even a bit inconsistent. I see this as a bug, but this may be a feature.  Where is the truth?

2) Explorer splits active states into: active and spilled. In particular, if you have N active states, max_active states will be "real" active, while N - max_active will be spilled. I am not 100%  sure but I don't see the concept of spilled states in PathGroup and thus all N active states will be explored. In my experiment, the memory usage of PathGroup when used like this:
```
pg = proj.factory.path_group(state) # the same if immutable=False
ex = pg.explore(find = XXX, avoid = YYY)
````
was much higher than Explorer. I had to kill angr when it was using ~4GB, while Explorer used at most ~1.2GB. Explorer is prioritizing (by default!) few active paths based on the instruction counter, while PathGroup is exploring any active state. On my workstation with 8GB of memory, Explorer is more "clever and efficient" than PathGroup. I know that this is may not be true in a general setting, 

My feeling is that PathGroup is much more flexible and powerful than Explorer, but it needs a bit of manual tuning. E.g., avoid PathGroup.explore() but use PathGroup.step() with some user defined "selector_func".

As it is, PathGroup.explore() is not an "equivalent" replacement for Explorer. Again, I am not sure if this  is a design choice or something that may be improved over time.  

Am I missing something about the usage of PathGroup? In particular, how can we "effectively" use it without adding some additional bits?

issue_title: Support specifying consistency level of the Cassandra session
issue_body:
While most of the time it is desirable to run DDL statements with the strongest consistency possible, in some cases it might be necessary to create a keyspace on a partially configured cluster. Very large clusters may not also deal well with a consistency level of ALL.

There should be an option in `cassandra-migrate.yml` to specify which consistency level to use by default in the `Session`.

issue_title: Running tests in different envs
issue_body:
Hi all,

Recently a minor(ish) bug had cropped up which effected users not running the bash shell as their environment.
I think the actual bug was due to different behaviour of bash when executed atop another bash session vs bash being executed atop a different shell. (I've not confirmed this but it seem to fit)

I think it would be a good idea to run the tests at least nightly atop a different shell such as 'zsh' or 'tclsh' to avoid the possibility of us being effected by assumptions in the code which may be not be valid.
e.g. executing a bash script within the same (non-bash) shell as the environment.

What are people's thoughts on this?

Thanks,

Rob

issue_body: Currently there is no way to control the lifetime of a proxy created by ganga. Only the default 24hours is possible. Proxies with much longer lifetimes can be made by the underlying Dirac tools, so it would be very useful if ganga could expose this throughs he DiracProxy class.

issue_title: job.copy has some slight problems
issue_body:
performing a job.copy on a job which is in a ```completed``` state will potentially give a bad ```outputfiles``` section in the new job.

For a completed job with ```*.txt``` for instance as a namePattern the completed job contains the resolved file from this job and a copy will return a new file which is fully resolved in the ```outputfiles``` which makes sense in terms of copying the actual value of the ```outputfiles``` list.

Should we consider making the ```outputfiles``` empty for jobs which are copying from ```completed``` jobs and inform the user that this is being erased?

This isn't something too serious but it would cause a ```job.copy().submit()``` to fail which is a tad irritating.

issue_title: Objects incorrectly being set dirty
issue_body:
LHCb users are seeing what I saw that some objects are marked as dirty when they should never be loaded.

The API for the ```_setDirty``` also doesn't respect ```_setDirty(False)``` which is a slight annoyance. If we're changing to use ```_setDirty()``` and ```_setFlushed()``` we should not respect the old API.

I'm tracking down what is causing some jobs to be loaded on shutdown which we thought was fixed at some level. (It appears this may only be fixed for simple jobs)

issue_body:

You may want to spawn several issues out of all this, your call.

> Note I don't use sudo as it's not mandatory using docker when properly configured.

To retrieve the `kinto.ini` configuration file, I'm doing:

```
$ mkdir config
$ docker-compose exec web cat /etc/kinto/kinto.ini > config/kinto.ini
```

I enabled the admin by updating the related section:

```
kinto.includes = kinto.plugins.default_bucket
                 kinto.plugins.admin
```

And updated the `docker-compose.yml` file to mount config to a local volume:

```
db:
  image: postgres
  environment:
    POSTGRES_USER: postgres
    POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
cache:
  image: library/memcached
web:
  image: kinto/kinto-server
  links:
  - db
  - cache
  ports:
  - "8888:8888"
  volumes:

    KINTO_CACHE_BACKEND: kinto.core.cache.memcached

    KINTO_PERMISSION_BACKEND: kinto.core.permission.postgresql
    KINTO_PERMISSION_URL: postgres://postgres:postgres@db/postgres
```

Then restarted the server.

The root endpoint loads fine at `http://127.0.0.1:8888`, thoug I noticed errors in the docker console:

```
web_1    | "GET   /?" 307 (3 ms)  agent=Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/68.0.3440.106 Safari/537.36 errno=0 lang=fr-FR,fr;q=0.9,en-US;q=0.8,en;q=0.7 time=2018-08-14T12:10:26.610000

web_1    | Connection <connection object at 0x7fa1383f22a8; dsn: 'user=postgres password=xxx dbname=postgres host=db', closed: 0> rollback-on-return, via agent

Though the admin loads fine at `http://127.0.0.1:8888/admin`, and I'm presented with a login form. I chose `Basic Auth`, entered test:test. This is the UI I'm facing right now:

![](https://i.imgur.com/LcmZC7Y.png)

There's a `bucket` bucket listed in the sidebar. When inspecting its properties by clicking on the gear icon next to it, I see this:

![](https://i.imgur.com/jZ2da6v.png)

Neither I can't list its collections:

![](https://i.imgur.com/5wBbpMZ.png)

So while this bucket might have a reason to exist technically, from a UX perspective it's really intriguing adn confusing; why is it here? Why is it useful for? Why can't I do anything with it?

Now on the other hand, I can create a new bucket just fine, and collections in it.

Now I click the Logout button. I'm now facing this screen:

issue_title: unicode bug in DAVIDenrich
issue_body:
From `AGEpy/AGEpy.py` in `DAVIDenrich` at line [427](https://github.com/mpg-age-bioinformatics/AGEpy/blob/master/AGEpy/AGEpy.py#L427)
```
for f in david_fields:
    line.append(str(d[f]))
```

the `str` call fails:

```
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\ufffd' in position 19: ordinal not in range(128)
```

One solution is to convert to unicode and envode as ascii, as in:

```
for f in david_fields:
    line.append(unicode(d[f]).encode('ascii','ignore'))
```

issue_title: RadialDistributionFunction featurizer should take a structure as an argument
API should allow multiple requests to be stubbed at the same time
issue_body:
Multiple interactions should be allowed to be set up at the same time. The /delete call should be called before the test, not before each interaction is set up. 

See this example using bash here: https://gist.github.com/bethesque/9d81f21d6f77650811f4

https://github.com/pact-foundation/pact-python/blob/master/pact/pact.py#L119

```python
def setup(self):
        """Configure the Mock Service to ready it for a test."""
        try:
            payload = {
                'description': self._description,
                'provider_state': self._provider_state,
                'request': self._request,
                'response': self._response
            }

            resp = requests.delete(
                self.uri + '/interactions', headers=self.HEADERS)

            assert resp.status_code == 200, resp.content
            resp = requests.post(
                self.uri + '/interactions',
                headers=self.HEADERS, json=payload)

            assert resp.status_code == 200, resp.content
        except AssertionError:
            raise
```

issue_title: Perform post upgrade checks/verification using a Pulp Smash script

Our existing upgrade process relies on a shell [script](https://github.com/pulp/pulp_packaging/blob/master/ci/jobs/scripts/post-upgrade.sh) that calls `pulp-admin` to loop through some expected content [populated](https://github.com/pulp/pulp_packaging/blob/master/ci/jobs/pulp-upgrade.yaml) by a prior task. However, there is no reliable way to use `pulp-admin` to assert that the content populated prior to the upgrade is actually still available and migrated to the newer version.

It would be great to write a Python script which can import Pulp Smash to perform these post upgrade checks and therefore increase our trust in the upgrade jobs.

issue_title: Django integration proposal
issue_body:
I've been thinking a bit about Django integration for Graphene. This is in part because I think it could be really useful, and in part because this is something I'd be interested in contributing a pull request for. However, development seems to be moving quickly so I thought it best to discuss these ideas first in case there are already plans.

The main motivation here is reducing boilerplate code. My intention would be to make all automatic functionality overridable & customisable.

**Nodes:**

-  ~~Automatically traverse Django relations. A `resolve_FIELD()` *may* be specified to customise this traversal, but if not then traversal will be done using the relation's manager's `all()` method.~~ [already supported]
- [x] Automatically support query args for all/specified model fields (reduces `resolve_FIELD()` boilerplate code).

**Query:**

- [x] Automatically resolve `ConnectionFields` which connect to `DjangoNodes` without the need for a `resolve_FIELD()` method.
- [ ] Support for pulling in schema definitions from individual Django apps, rather than being defined centrally. (I currently do this by using a top-level `Query` class which inherits from multiple app-specific `Query` classes) [related: #55]

**Mutations:**

- [ ] Provide a `DjangoMutation` which provides standard functionality though which to update Django models.
- [ ] Consider a class-based views approach, with `CreateMutation`, `UpdateMutation`, and `DeleteMutation` base classes.

All/any thoughts very welcome indeed!

issue_title: Need clarification of purpose for `pure_c` option
issue_body:
The description of this option is here: 

> pure_c: (Default True) If your project is C++, pass the pure_c=False, it will add new build combinations using libstdc and libstdc++11 in the `setting compiler.libcxx`.

By default, `setting compiler.libcxx` is not used for hashing/ABI compatibility, so binaries built with `setting compiler.libcxx=libstdc` and `setting compiler.libcxx=libstdc++11` are identical, correct? 

I believe the primary purpose of conan-package-tools is to build the wide range of *different* binaries, so that users don't have to re-build themselves.  Since setting `pure_c=False` effectively doubles the number of builds on Linux and OSX, but half of the binaries are identical by default, then it would seem to be a bit redundant/wasteful to use this option. 

I can see one possible value proposition for this however, which is testing.  If someone wants to build their packages against both `stdc++` libraries under various compilers to make sure it works, then this makes sense.  Is this the primary purpose of exposing this option in conan-package-tools, or have I got something else wrong?

issue_title: Feature request: give new parameter to a tf.summary.scalar to indicate which graph it belongs to
issue_body:
I have stumbled upon the difficulties of plotting more plots into the same graph using tf.summary.scalar during one session run. I, like many others, want to plot a train and validation curve on the same graph, but have only seen workarounds to this, where two different filewriters create two different directories for saving the two different plots. This is indeed a workaround, and sometimes it does not even work - for example when I want to save a tf.summary.scalar with the same name for train and validation set during one run. Another example that this workaround doesn't work on, as I saw someone else point out in issue #300 , is the A3C algorithm.

I have read this thread and seen that there is a tensorboard plugin (tensorboard/plugins/custom_scalar) that also is a workaround to the issue; a plugin that I, unfortunately, haven't managed to get to work properly. I wanted to mention this to show that I am aware that this plugin exists, and to point out that I am looking for a different solution.

The solution I want is to make it possible to provide a parameter to tf.summary.scalar to indicate which graph it belongs to. The tf.summary.scalar would then have both a unique identifier in the name parameter, along with a graph identifier in the graph_name parameter. The complete function would then look like this:

tf.summary.scalar(name, tensor, graph_name, collections, family)

Is this possible?

```patch
From: Andrew Shadura <andrew.shadura@collabora.co.uk>
Date: Wed, 12 Oct 2016 18:04:31 +0100
Subject: Install syntax highlighter files correctly.

--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -336,7 +336,9 @@
                      'VERSION.txt'],
           'hotdoc.utils': ['hotdoc.m4', 'hotdoc.mk'],

+              'prism/CNAME',
+              'prism/*.*',
               'prism/components/*',
               'prism/themes/*',
               'prism/plugins/autoloader/*',
```

I apologize for adding three specific feature requests to a single issue, but since they're all related, and there's a corresponding screen shot, I thought it might be best here.

Also, I like the idea of the tool emitting Markdown compatible files, but since it's new and unexpected behavior, I would advocate that one needs a flag to explicitly enable it.

# Screenshot
Top screen `jasmine` is running an older version of `csvlook`. By inspecting the file `/usr/local/bin/version` I think this is csvkit 0.9.1. Fails when trying to use unknown flag ``--no-inference` instead of ignoring the flag.

Bottom screen `belle` doesn't have a version, and it's not clear from inspecting the python source. But it's recent as of yesterday. 

The CSV file in question is 511 rows x 546 cols (a mix of strings, ints, floats and booleans)

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/280700/22307094/988bf48e-e2f6-11e6-8fb0-fc6cb14e9bb4.png)

# My requests
1. Add `--version`. It'll be hard to report bugs if there's not an easy way to query what version is installed. Also, if there was a reliable `--version` flag, then we could query at shell login and set appropriate wrappers to enable `

2. Make `--no-inference` the default state. So one would use `--inference` to enable it.

3. Inference is *slow*. In the second screenshot, it takes 10 seconds to complete, vs. 1 second disabled. The older version (in `jasmine`) was even faster at 1/2 second.

I love the tool...I probably use `csvlook` 50-100 times *per day*. Across 6 machines (I do a lot with CSV files). 

# My hack solution
Fork `csvkit` and disable inference by default. If I get this working, I'll submit pull request, but it would be a surprise to your new users that _expect_ the inference to be enabled by default. 

Maybe there's an *environment* variable that can control all this?

Due to many changes to csvkit and, in parallel, to dependencies (like agate), it is not easy to make a working installation out of git checkout.

I suggest to add a portion of docs to "Tips and troubleshooting" considering the installation of csvkit and relatives from git, using virtualenv and either `pip install git+<repourl>` or `git clone <repourl>; pip install -r <reqfile>`. Plus, adding the link to such a doc from README to allow for people visiting Github repo to quickly jump on working on the library.

issue_title: Investigate replacing optparse with argparse, docopt, or click
issue_body:
optparse has been deprecated since Python 3.2 and all development on it has ceased:
https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/optparse.html

We should investigate replacing the usage of optparse with argparse or a similar library such as docopt or click.

Since the APIs aren't identical, it won't be a trivial drop-in replacement.

issue_title: Feature request: retain DataFrame index when `df_out=True`
issue_body: Right now if you ask for a DataFrame output with `df_out=True`, you lose the information in the DataFrame's index. Since Sklearn transformers may not add or remove rows, it makes sense to retain the input DataFrame's index when `df_out=True`.

issue_title: SERVFAIL with bind9 (apparmor and bind issue, really, the solution needs to be added to the documentation)
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 18.04 LTS

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
OS package manager & certbot-dns-rfc2136 with pip

## The Issue and the Solution
I've tried to issue a wildcard certificate with certbot-dns-rfc2136 and bind, but it didn't work because of SERVFAIL error the server was returning. All the settings were pretty much identical to what you can read in certbot-dns-rfc2136's documentation, so I just skip right past them. The solution was to set the permissions for /etc/bind to 774, since bind could not create a journal file in that directory and set access mode to rwix for /etc/bind/** in /etc/apparmor.d/usr.sbin.named, since apparmor was blocking it too.

## The Reason Why
I don't really know how to edit the documentation and I don't have any ideas about it, so if anyone could add this to the documentation, that would have been wonderful.

issue_title: PyTA checkers name typos and inconsistency bewteen message symbols
issue_body:
There are some typos in the names of assigning_to_self_checker.py and invalid_range_index_checker.py  

```
class AssigningToSelfChecker(BaseChecker):

__implements__ = IAstroidChecker

name = 'always_return'  

class InvalidRangeIndexChecker(BaseChecker):
__implements__ = IAstroidChecker

name = 'custom'
```

The naming style is not consistent across all PyTA checkers. Underscores are used in message symbols in some checkers while dashes are used in others. The same inconsistency happens to the names of checkers too.

issue_title: Handle large result sets
issue_body:
### Problem
Large result sets (100K+) cause `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object` due to [this recursion](https://github.com/simple-salesforce/simple-salesforce/blob/master/simple_salesforce/api.py#L407).

### Solution
* Use a non recursive implementation to get results
* Use a generator to process results in small batches rather than holding the entire result set in memory

issue_title: Query Logically Deleted Records
issue_body:
Hi Love Simple Salesforce.  Thank you for it.

Is there a way to query Logically deleted records?  In other words can it do this:

SELECT Id FROM Contact WHERE isDeleted=true and masterRecordId != null 
  AND SystemModstamp > 2006-01-01T23:01:01+01:00

This query is what Salesforce suggests to know when two records have been merged.

While the simple-salesforce deleted() method returns a list of ids deleted, it does not give the masterRecordId.

The Salesforce restful api supports a 'query_all' to get logically deleted records. However simple-salesforce's query_all is a wrapper around query() and query_more()

Thank you,
David Link
New York

issue_title: review FIFO-Queue for Event emission (from API to client)
issue_body:
**Note**: this is partially implemented in [PR #280](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py), but needs improvement. Here is an explanaition of the code:

The API will pass a flat list of Events to the client via the `get_new_events()` [method](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py#L463-L480), which can be polling or long-polling (for long polling see link above).

The Events include all the [blockchain-events](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py#L79-L84) for the channel, as well as a [TransferReceived](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py#L88) event for incoming messages that represent some kind of transfer.

Blockchain events will be constructed in the [same place](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py#L859-L864) where they get passed to raiden's internals for processing via a callback.
(We cannot just simply pass blockchain events, because the emitting contract address should be included in raiden's events.)

The TransferReceived event get's constructed via the [on_withdrawable](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py#L106-L108) callbacks.

Once the callbacks are registered in those places, the events will be [automatically put](https://github.com/ezdac/raiden/blob/ea1941af7c866a8fd61fcb2a897482f0ab6d026f/raiden/raiden_service.py#L117) in the Event-Queue.

**To Do**:

- Please review the above concept and provide some input.
- The Queue needs some limitation (max-size), otherwise it will get filled indefinitely when no client polls events
- the callback registration/ filling of the Queue should depend on some kind of `raiden --api --events=True` flag
- provide a simple polling option for the  `get_new_events` method  (trivial)

**Assumptions / Consequences of above concept**:

- the client needs to poll for an initial state on connect (events will only serve as state-updates during the connect)
- Events will only be saved until the queue is full and as long as the node is running

issue_title: DICOM export unique UID
issue_body:
Eclipse (and probably other TPSes) assumes that every new CT scan and every new VOI DICOM has new UID. After opening any DICOM in Eclipse it loads it and keeps it in its database for future reference.

Right now pyTRiP98 saves fixed UIDs to exported DICOMs. It means if you load in Eclipse DICOM file exported from pyTRiP then first file will load correctly. But when you take i.e. different CT cube, export it to DICOM then Eclipse import will fail - Eclipse will complain that DICOM with same UID already exists.

The way to solve that problem would be to generate unique UIDs, based for example on hashes from CTX and HED data. I think we should still assume that two identical CTX+HED sets leads to the same UIDs - generating UIDs based on timestamps doesn't make sense.

issue_title: Calling magics from sub kernels.
issue_body:
I am not quite sure how to execute magics from subkernels but we should perhaps allow passing magics to subkernels if we are to keep maximum compatibility with them. Then we should adjust our magic syntax to accommodate this. For example, right now

```
%magic1

%magic2
statement
```
are all sos magics, perhaps we should require that sos magics to start from the first line and without newline, and other magics should be separated form sos magics by an empty newline. That is to say, in the above example, we can treat `%magic1` as sos magic, and `%magic2` as subkernel magic.

Of course we will need to first make sure we could call magics of subkernels.

issue_title: to_host does not work remotely.
issue_body:
For example

```
task:  on_host='machine', path_map='/home/user:/Users/user', to_host=input
sh:
   touch ${to_host(output)} 
```

Option `to_host=input` works because its how to expand `input` to full path name and convert to dest name. However, `to_host` function is executed on the remote machine and does not know how to expand `output` to full path name (relative to local machine), so the path translation would fail.

The solution I think is to 
1. pass all variables `input`, `output` etc as full path name to remote machine (so that the translation can be done). ...
2. perform `to_host` calculation locally

issue_title: Don't rerun same task with same arguments if result is already available
issue_body:
Currently it looks like every call to `.remote(...)` starts a new task no matter if the result for the same task and same arguments already exist. Ideally, for pure tasks, results could be reused instead of recomputed.

Is such memoization something which is aligned with Ray approach? Or should we build it on top of Ray with another layer of abstraction? I worry that in the latter case there would be additional latency to check another database for potential "arguments -> object" mappings which already exist.

issue_title: Youtube Live stream is playing up to 5 minutes before session.streams() stops returning None
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [x] This is a bug report.
- [ ] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [x ] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description
Platform: Raspberry Pi 3 - Stretch
Encoder: Adobe Flash Media Live Encoder 3.2
Encoder Setup URL I: As per Youtube recommendations for 720p video H264 streams.
Encoder Setup URL II: 30 fps, 320x240 USB2.0 Webcam, 500Kbps, H264 streams.
Streamlink: 0.10.0
Python: 2.7
URL I: https://youtube.com/user/ApostolicFaithHQ/live
Live Sundays 11AM and 6PM, Tues and Fri 7:30PM
URL II: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6Q5UfqUUsmsW59m8xZSc8Q/live
Injection site for test. Time is intermittent throughout the day.

Code flow overview:
Python script uses the Streamlink API to query either of the URLs above every 15 seconds until session.streams() returns other than None.  The script then searches for a certain key in the streams object  to determine if the stream is Live or is something Youtube seems to put at the URL when a recent Live video has ended and at the time it is stored to the video archive.  If the key is not found the process repeats indefinitely.
When the key is found, the script launches a subprocess.Popen to start a CLI version of Streamlink with omxplayer (because I am a bit of a NOOB and haven't figured out how to stream to omxplayer with the API).  When the Live stream has ended, the python script does some cleanup and repeats the infinite query.

Use Case A: Happy
The video stream has been started for sometime _before_ streamlink/python script is started.  The query of the URL at 15 second intervals, 'finds' a live stream within 30 seconds (sooner if the interval timer is reduced). The video plays perfectly for the entire session without freeze, jitter, etc.

Use Case B: Sad
The video stream is started any time _after_ the streamlink/python script begins its query of the URL at 15 second intervals.  In this case, session.streams() returns None for upto 5.5 minutes after the video stream has started.  Once the video finally starts, it plays perfectly for the entire session without freeze, jitter, etc.

In either Use Case, a browser (on Windows 7) will begin showing the live stream immediately.

### Expected / Actual behavior
The expectation is that Use Case B stops returning None within a few seconds of a Live stream starting.

### Reproduction steps / Explicit stream URLs to test
1. Start the streamlink/python script before the Live stream begins, using <URL> <best> as parameters.
2. Start the Live stream using the Encoder noted above..
3. Observe that a typical interval before session.streams() returns data other than None is 2.5 to 5.5 minutes.

### Logs
[afc_streamer_Sad.log](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/1746333/afc_streamer_Sad.log)
[afc_streamer_Happy.log](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/1746334/afc_streamer_Happy.log)
Python Script

[GitHub Bug Filing.zip](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/1746376/GitHub.Bug.Filing.zip)

### Comments, screenshots, etc.



[Love Streamlink? Please consider supporting our collective. Thanks!](https://opencollective.com/streamlink/donate)

issue_title: on_conflict/upsert does not allow `pk` as a valid conflict target
issue_body:
```
class MyModel(psqlextra.Model):
     title = models.CharField(max_length=14)

MyMode.objects.on_conflict(['pk'], ConflictAction.UPDATE).insert(pk=0, title='great')
```

Raises the following exception:

```
django.core.exceptions.SuspiciousOperation: pk is not a valid conflict target, specify a list of column names, or tuples with column names and hstore key.
```

In Django, `pk` is a special keyword that always refers to the primary key of a model. `psqlextra/compiler.py` should also look at `model._meta.pk` when validating conflict targets.

issue_title: Windows compatibility
issue_body:
Windows may not be the OS of choice for programmers but for other scientists and students it is often the OS to go. There is a problem in `requests_file` with local files on a windows machine. It can not handle the windows OS.sep becaus the url decoding is implemented with this statement: 

`path_parts = [unquote(p) for p in url_parts.path.split('/')] `.

Maybe this is not the most urgent 'bug' to handle. But it is a problem that we should take care of.

issue_title: dataset add doing more work than needed
issue_body:
### Problem Description

Handling of lineage data is in-effecient.

Lineage is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), but since yaml/json can only represent trees, it is stored on disk as a tree with duplicates. These duplicates are processed multiple times by `dataset add/update`.

Example: DAG on the left will be stored on disk as a tree shown on the right, with datasets C,D,E being duplicated.

```
A--> B              |      A--> B --> C' --> D' --> E'
|    |              |>     |
|    v              |>     +--> C --> D --> E 
+--> C--> D -> E    |
```

Which in itself is a problem and we should probably support special "reference/pointer" type of serialisation to avoid storing the same data multiple times on disk (it's not just space concern it's more about duplication that might lead to inconsistency).

Rather than converting the tree back into a DAG, `dataset add` processes the tree directly, visiting and processing datasets `C`,`D`,`E` multiple times, this involves

1. Looking them up in the DB
2. Building python representation object
3. Checking metadata consistency (possibly involves second lookup for the same uuid again)

It's a fairly costly procedure and negatively impacts new product development life-cycle, whole working day spent on indexing into a test database is not uncommon.

issue_title: How to set Jaeger endpoint
DynamoDB::createTable with `StreamEnabled: true` hangs
issue_body: When running locally, I've noticed that if I try to create a dynamodb table _with_ streams enabled, I get a network timeout, but if the stream is disabled I get a table created right away. Interestingly, the table appears to be created — the NodeJS client is just never informed of it. Steps to Reproduce ================== Start up the container: ```bash docker run -it \ -e SERVICES="dynamodb,dynamodbstreams" \ -p 4569:4569 \ -p 4570:4570 \ -p 8080:8080 \ -e DATA_DIR="/tmp/localstack/data" \ -v ${HOME}/data/localstack:/tmp/localstack \ localstack/localstack ``` Browse to [the web shell](http://localhost:4569/shell/) for DynamoDB. Try to create a DynamoDB table with streams _disabled_: ```javascript var params = { TableName: 'test-without-executions', KeySchema: [ { AttributeName: 'id', KeyType: 'HASH' } ], AttributeDefinitions: [ { AttributeName: 'id', AttributeType: 'S' } ], ProvisionedThroughput: { ReadCapacityUnits: 10, WriteCapacityUnits: 10 }, StreamSpecification: { StreamEnabled: false } }; dynamodb.createTable(params, function(err, data) { if (err) ppJson(err); // an error occurred else ppJson(data); // successful response }); ``` and celebrate, for the table is created! No logs from docker. Next, try to create a _very similar_ table with streams _enabled_: ```javascript var params = { TableName: 'test-with-executions', KeySchema: [ { AttributeName: 'id', KeyType: 'HASH' } ], AttributeDefinitions: [ { AttributeName: 'id', AttributeType: 'S' } ], ProvisionedThroughput: { ReadCapacityUnits: 10, WriteCapacityUnits: 10 }, StreamSpecification: { StreamEnabled: true, StreamViewType: 'NEW_IMAGE' } }; dynamodb.createTable(params, function(err, data) { if (err) ppJson(err); // an error occurred else ppJson(data); // successful response }); ``` and weep - the network console hangs and eventually delivers the following error: Again, no logs from the docker side of things. The table appears to have been created successfully :confused:?

issue_title: GroupConv implement
issue_body:
`class GroupConv(Layer):
    def __init__(
        self,
        layer = None,
        filters=0,
        size=3,
        stride=2,
        groups=2,
        act = tf.identity,
        name ='groupconv',
    ):
        Layer.__init__(self, name=name)
        self.inputs = layer.outputs
        groupConv = lambda i, k: tf.nn.conv2d(i, k, strides=[1, stride, stride, 1], padding='VALID')
        channels = int(self.inputs.get_shape()[-1])
        with tf.variable_scope(name) as vs:
            We = tf.get_variable(name='weights', shape=[size, size, channels / groups, filters],
                                       initializer=tf.truncated_normal_initializer(stddev=0.03),
                                       dtype=tf.float32, trainable=True)
            bi = tf.get_variable(name='biases', shape=[filters, ],
                                      initializer=tf.constant_initializer(value=0.1),
                                      dtype=tf.float32, trainable=True)
        if groups == 1:
            conv = groupConv(self.inputs, We)
        else:
            inputGroups = tf.split(axis=3, num_or_size_splits=groups, value=self.inputs)
            weightsGroups = tf.split(axis=3, num_or_size_splits=groups, value=We)
            convGroups = [groupConv(i, k) for i, k in zip(inputGroups, weightsGroups)]
            conv = tf.concat(axis=3, values=convGroups)
        if name is not None:
            conv = tf.add(conv, bi, name=name)
        else:
            conv = tf.add(conv, bi)
        self.outputs =act(conv)
        self.all_layers = list(layer.all_layers)
        self.all_params = list(layer.all_params)
        self.all_drop = dict(layer.all_drop)
        self.all_layers.extend( [self.outputs] )
        self.all_params.extend( [We, bi] )`

Caffe中分组卷积的实现，简单说就是比如输入224x224x4的图像用，32个filters，分2groups，就是分2个224x224x2图，然后用32个filters去卷积，生成俩32层的tensor然后concat到一起，具体类似caffe。一个减少过拟合的卷积设计。

issue_title: V0.3.2 版本正式发布啦！！！
issue_body:
# pyecharts V0.3.2 发布日志

> pyecharts V0.3.2 发布，这是一个重大更新的版本。

## 1 全面重构地图加载方式

从本版本开始，为了缩减项目本身的体积以及维持 pyecharts 项目的轻量化运行，pyecharts 将不再自带地图 js 文件。如用户需要用到地图图表，可自行安装对应的地图文件包。下面介绍如何安装。

地图文件被分成了三个 Python 包，分别为

1. [全球国家地图](https://echarts-maps.github.io/echarts-countries-js/): [echarts-countries-pypkg](https://github.com/pyecharts/echarts-countries-pypkg) (1.9MB)
2. [中国省级地图](https://echarts-maps.github.io/echarts-china-provinces-js/): [echarts-china-provinces-pypkg](https://github.com/pyecharts/echarts-china-provinces-pypkg) (730KB)
3. [中国市级地图](https://echarts-maps.github.io/echarts-china-cities-js/): [echarts-china-cities-pypkg](https://github.com/pyecharts/echarts-china-cities-pypkg) (3.8MB)

安装方式也很简单，可以仅选择所需要的包，也可以全部安装
```
$ pip install echarts-countries-pypkg
$ pip install echarts-china-provinces-pypkg
$ pip install echarts-china-cities-pypkg
```

地图包由 [lml](https://github.com/chfw/lml) 负责管理和识别， lml 是一个松散的包管理工具，有兴趣的同学可以前往了解。三个包也同时会将 js 文件带入到 notebook 环境中去。


## 2 恢复 online 函数的使用

由于考虑到不少用户有将 notebook 保存为 html 文件的做法，所以恢复 online 函数的使用，使用户 .html 即使脱离 notebook 环境也可以挂载到 Github host，确保图形可以正常显示。


## 3 为 Kline 提供 Candlestick 别名

现在可以引入 Candlestick 类
```
from pyecharts import Candlestick
candlestick = Candlestick("K 线图-自定义标记点风格")
candlestick.add("日K", DATE, data, mark_point=["min", "max"],
                mark_point_symbolsize=80,
                datazoom_orient='vertical',
                mark_line_valuedim=['lowest', 'highest'])
candlestick.render()
```

## 4 加入 AppVeyor CI

一直以来 pyecharts 都只有 Travis CI，而 Travis 又只是针对 Linux 测试，现在加入了 AppVeyor CI，提供了 Windows 平台的测试功能。

## 5 pyecharts/assets 

自本版本开始，将不再为 Flask/Django/.. 等 WEB 框架提供 DEFAULT_HOST，但我们为开发者新建了 pyecharts/assets 库挂载 pyecharts 所需要的所有 js 文件，可以使用下面语句替换原来的 DEFAULT_HOST
```
REMOTE_HOST = "https://pyecharts.github.io/assets/js"
```


## 5 修复了若干 BUG
* [issue#322](https://github.com/pyecharts/pyecharts/issues/322) 修复在 timeline 中不能设置多个 legend 的 bug
* [issue#357](https://github.com/pyecharts/pyecharts/issues/357) 修复 Line 图 symbol 大小不能调整的 bug
* [issue#371](https://github.com/pyecharts/pyecharts/issues/371) 修复 Parallel 图 Line 样式失效的 bug
* [issue#378](https://github.com/pyecharts/pyecharts/issues/378) 修复 Geo 图中当多次 render 时相同 value 值会被叠加的 bug
* [issue#338](https://github.com/pyecharts/pyecharts/issues/338) 修复 timeline 中 map 的 visualmap 组件不能正常显示的 bug

更多细节内容可移步至 [changelog](http://pyecharts.org/#/zh-cn/changelog) 做进一步了解

I am trying to use the space 'leven'. Here is a toy example of a failed attempt.

```
import nmslib
import numpy as np
import random

f = 4 # length of vectors
seqs = []
for i in xrange(400):
    v = [random.randint(0, 2) for z in xrange(f)]
    seqs.append(''.join(map(str,v)))

seqs = np.array(seqs)
index = nmslib.init(method='hnsw', space='leven')
index.addDataPointBatch(seqs)
index.createIndex()
ids, distances = index.knnQuery(seqs[0], k=2)
```

This gives:

> RuntimeError: 2018-07-28 08:58:41 spacefactory.h:50 (CreateSpace) It looks like the space leven is not defined for the distance type : FLOAT

I also tried:

`seqs.append(v)`

with the same failure.

What is the right way to do this?

issue_title: Client Certificate CA
issue_body: Is there a way to specify a CA certificate when initializing client to go along with Certificate and Private Key in the `cert` tuple? The Absence of a way to specify CA file is a deal breaker for this library unfortunately.

issue_title: SyntaxError in rdflib/plugins/serializers/nt.py with Python 2.4
issue_body:
dan...@gmail.com, 2011-07-28T02:56:34.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Run rdflib-3.1.0 tests with Python 2.4

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?

  File "/usr/lib/python2.4/site-packages/rdflib/plugins/serializers/nt.py", line 47
    chars.append('\u%04X' % ord(char) if ord(char) <= 0xFFFF else '\U%08X' % ord(char))
                                       ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
rdflib 3.1.0 with Python 2.4

Patch attached.

Comment 1 by gromgull
Thanks! I'll apply it - is this really the only thing that is not py2.4 compatible in rdflib? I think we gave up trying to support 2.4 a while back.

Our application is still running on Python 2.4 (RHEL5), so I'll be supporting rdflib on Python 2.4 even if the rdflib developers aren't :-)

With this patch applied to 3.1.0 all rdflib tests pass, as do the tests for our application, although we aren't running rdflib 3.1.0 in production yet. We have been using 3.0.0 successfully in production for some time now.

This issue was closed by revision 91ca3aef3477.

issue_title: require() returns single-element array
issue_body:
When I do something like `var c = require('config.yaml');` I get a single-element array with the YAML-derived data structure as the element. I guess that's more or less as documented, but it seems strange. I would have expected that I'd get just the data structure, with no extra array around it. Having to use a `[0]` to get to the actual data is an annoyance. Is there some point I'm missing?

That is, I'd hoped to get

    {"property1":"value1","property2":"value2"}

but instead I get

    [{"property1":"value1","property2":"value2"}]

issue_title: Full unit test coverage of the futures manager.
Do diffs detect renamed files properly?
issue_body:
```python
diff = commit.diff(*parents, R = True, M = True)```

I'm using following code for detecting changes between commits. Everything works ok in except of renames. When I use ```M = True``` it should detect renames instead of add / deletions. However, when I use it, it doesn't return anything. Is this a bug or did I just misunderstand how to use it?
